questions.question: What is the correct command to set a DCE clock rate of 56K on a serial interface?
questions.explanation: The clock rate command is entered in interface config mode. The value is in bits per second, so 56000 = 56 Kbps. The command syntax is 'clock rate' (two words), not 'clockrate'.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q3; Cisco IOS Command Reference

questions.question: What is the correct command to set the bandwidth of a serial interface to 56K?
questions.explanation: The 'bandwidth' command in interface config mode takes a value in kilobits per second. For 56K, the value is 56 (not 56000). This is used for routing metric calculations — it does not change actual interface speed.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q4; Cisco IOS Command Reference

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 'line console 0' command?
options: Establish a Telnet session with a remote router
Set a login password on the console terminal
Set a login password for an incoming Telnet session
Set a password to restrict access to privileged EXEC mode
questions.explanation: The 'line console 0' command enters line configuration mode for the physical console port. From here you set the console login password and enable login authentication.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q5; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 8

questions.question: RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol) is best described as:
options: A host resolves its IP by broadcasting its MAC address; a RARP server assigns an IP
A host resolves the MAC address of a destination by sending a broadcast
A host resolves a destination IP by broadcasting on the local network
RARP is used only on Token Ring networks
questions.explanation: RARP is the reverse of ARP. A diskless workstation that knows only its MAC address broadcasts to a RARP server to obtain an IP address. Largely superseded by DHCP.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q6 and 98-10 Q3; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: At what OSI layer does ICMP operate?
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) operates at Layer 3 (Network). It is encapsulated inside IP packets and is used for error reporting and diagnostic functions like ping and traceroute.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q4; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 3

questions.question: Which of the following are benefits of segmenting a network with routers? (Choose three)
questions.explanation: Routers create separate broadcast AND collision domains on each interface, increasing per-node bandwidth and controlling broadcasts. They do not increase frame size.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q6; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 1

questions.question: IP subnetting provides which primary benefits? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: Subnetting allows a single network number to be divided into smaller logical networks, conserving address space and enabling hierarchical routing.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q7; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: Which device operates at Layer 2 of the OSI model and uses MAC addresses to forward frames?
questions.explanation: A switch operates at Layer 2 (Data Link) and builds a MAC address table (CAM table) to make forwarding decisions. A hub is Layer 1 and floods all ports.
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 6: Ethernet LAN Switching; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 5

questions.question: What is the size of an IPv4 address?
questions.explanation: IPv4 addresses are 32 bits (4 octets of 8 bits each), written in dotted-decimal notation (e.g., 192.168.1.1). IPv6 addresses are 128 bits.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 1, Chapter 7: IPv4 Addressing

questions.question: Which of the following is a valid Class B network address?
questions.explanation: Class B networks span 128.0.0.0–191.255.255.255. 172.16.0.0 is a private Class B range. 10.x is Class A, 192.168.x is Class C, 224.x is multicast.
questions.source_ref: Wendell Odom - CCNA 200-301 OCG Vol 1, Chapter 7

questions.question: A hub forwards frames to all ports regardless of the destination MAC address.
questions.explanation: Hubs are Layer 1 devices that broadcast all received data to every port — they have no MAC table and create a single collision domain.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q11; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 6

questions.question: Which protocol resolves an IP address to a MAC address on the local subnet?
questions.explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) sends a broadcast asking 'Who has IP x.x.x.x?' and the owner replies with its MAC address. ARP maps Layer 3 to Layer 2.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q5 and Q55; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 8: ARP

questions.question: What is the TFTP destination port number?
questions.explanation: TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol) uses UDP port 69. FTP uses TCP 20/21, SNMP uses UDP 161, SSH uses TCP 22.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q1; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: Which applications use TCP (connection-oriented) at Layer 4? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: Telnet (TCP 23) and FTP (TCP 20/21) use connection-oriented TCP. TFTP uses UDP 69 and SNMP uses UDP 161 — both are connectionless.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q7; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 3

questions.question: Which of the following are link-state routing protocols? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: OSPF and IS-IS are link-state protocols — they advertise link states to all routers in the area and each runs the SPF algorithm. RIP is distance-vector. EIGRP is advanced distance-vector (hybrid).
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q8 and Q23; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 20

questions.question: What SNMP port number does it use?
questions.explanation: SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) uses UDP port 161 for queries and 162 for traps. It operates at the Application layer.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q51; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the correct description of administrative distance as used in Cisco IOS?
options: The metric used to select the best path to a destination
The process where a router selects an administrator for the AS
The trustworthiness of a routing source — lower AD wins when protocols conflict
The maximum number of hops allowed in a routing domain
questions.explanation: Administrative Distance (AD) is Cisco's way of rating the trustworthiness of a routing information source. When two protocols advertise a route to the same destination, the lower AD wins. EIGRP=90, OSPF=110, RIP=120.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q10; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 16

questions.question: A caret symbol (^) in a failed Cisco IOS command indicates:
options: Numeric data is required at that position
Press Enter to complete the command
The point where an incorrect command, keyword, or argument was entered
A required value must be entered
questions.explanation: The ^ marks the position in the command string where IOS detected an error. Use 'sh ar?' (no space before ?) to see available commands beginning with 'ar'.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q33 and 98-09 Q7; Cisco IOS Fundamentals

questions.question: Which commands display the IP addresses of all interfaces on a Cisco router? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: 'show ip interface' and 'show interface' both display IP addressing information. 'show ip route' shows the routing table. 'show ip address' is not a valid command.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q4; Cisco IOS Command Reference

questions.question: What commands display the ARP cache on a Cisco router? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: Both 'show ip arp' and 'show arp' display the ARP table mapping IP addresses to MAC addresses. The table entries age out after 4 hours by default.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q5; Cisco IOS Command Reference

questions.question: When does a Cisco IOS configuration command take effect?
questions.explanation: Configuration changes in IOS take effect immediately and modify the running-config in RAM. They are NOT persistent until saved with 'write memory' or 'copy run start'.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q64; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 8

questions.question: Which commands display the running configuration? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: 'show running-config' (or 'show run') and the legacy 'write terminal' both display the current running configuration. 'show configuration' shows startup-config in older IOS versions.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q61; Cisco IOS Command Reference

questions.question: What does TTL (Time To Live) prevent in an IP network?
options: IP datagrams caught in a routing loop from traversing the network forever
questions.explanation: TTL is a counter in the IP header that each router decrements by 1. When it reaches 0, the packet is discarded. This prevents packets from looping endlessly due to routing misconfiguration.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q62; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: A MAC address consists of which two parts?
options: Broadcast domain ID and node ID
questions.explanation: A 48-bit MAC address consists of: first 24 bits = Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI/Vendor Code) assigned by IEEE; last 24 bits = unique serial number assigned by the vendor. Cisco OUI: 00:00:0C.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q54; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 2

questions.question: Features of TCP connection-oriented service include: (Choose all that apply)
questions.explanation: TCP is connection-oriented and provides: three-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK), flow control, error recovery, windowing, and sequencing. UDP provides none of these.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q49; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 3: TCP vs UDP

questions.question: In the first segment of the TCP three-way handshake, what is the ACK value?
options: The SEQ of the remote host + 1
questions.explanation: The first SYN segment of the three-way handshake has ACK=0 because no sequence number has been received yet. This is why 'established' in an ACL blocks session initiation — it only permits packets where ACK is non-zero.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q52; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What would NOT appear in a Cisco routing table?
questions.explanation: Routing tables operate at Layer 3 and contain IP addresses, next-hops, metrics, and egress interfaces. MAC addresses are Layer 2 and belong in the switch's MAC address (CAM) table.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q27; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 16

questions.question: How many usable subnets can be created from a Class C address with mask 255.255.255.252?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.252 = /30 uses 6 subnet bits in the 4th octet. Formula: 2^6 - 2 = 62 subnets (excluding all-zeros and all-ones subnets per legacy rules). Each subnet has 2 usable hosts.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q12; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: What subnet mask allows exactly 62 usable host addresses on a Class C network?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.192 = /26. Host bits = 6. Usable hosts = 2^6 - 2 = 62. The formula is 2^n - 2 where n = number of host bits.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q63; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: What is the subnet address for 193.243.12.43 with mask 255.255.255.128?
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q29; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: Which subnet mask is nicknamed the 'serial mask' because it provides exactly 2 host addresses?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.252 = /30 provides 2 usable hosts — exactly what is needed for a point-to-point serial link between two routers. Each /30 subnet = 4 addresses: network, 2 hosts, broadcast.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q35; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: Order the OSI layers from Layer 7 (top) to Layer 1 (bottom):
questions.explanation: OSI Layers 7→1: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical. Mnemonic (top-down): 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing'.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q40-48 (OSI series); Jeremy's IT Lab Day 2

questions.question: Match each PDU name to the correct OSI layer (order from Layer 4 down to Layer 1):
questions.explanation: Layer 4=Segments (TCP/UDP), Layer 3=Packets/Datagrams (IP), Layer 2=Frames (Ethernet), Layer 1=Bits. This encapsulation chain is how data travels down the OSI stack.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q45-48; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 2

questions.question: The size of a window in a TCP segment establishes what?
options: The maximum number of bytes a single frame may not exceed
The allowable bandwidth for this TCP session
The number of octets the receiver must be willing to accept
The number of octets the sender is willing to accept
questions.explanation: The TCP window size field specifies the number of octets the SENDER is willing to accept from the other side — it advertises the receiver's buffer capacity to the remote sender, enabling flow control.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q72; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3: TCP/IP Transport

questions.question: Which protocol is best described as connection-oriented and reliable?
questions.explanation: TCP (Transmission Control Protocol) at Layer 4 is connection-oriented (three-way handshake) and reliable (sequencing, ACKs, retransmission). UDP is connectionless and unreliable. IP is connectionless best-effort.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q73; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 3: OSI/TCP-IP Model

questions.question: A Telnet session to a remote host is addressed to what port number at the remote host?
questions.explanation: Telnet uses TCP port 23. The source port is ephemeral (>1023) assigned by the client OS. Other well-known ports: SSH=22, FTP=21, DNS=53, HTTP=80, HTTPS=443.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q74; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: TCP source port numbers are best described as:
options: Depend on the application being accessed on the destination host
Depend on the application running on the source host
Usually assigned by the source host and typically greater than 1023
Are assigned by the source and are usually one of the well-known port numbers
questions.explanation: Source ports are ephemeral ports (1024–65535) dynamically assigned by the client OS. Destination ports are well-known (0–1023). This allows a host to run multiple simultaneous TCP sessions.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q75; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: In a TCP acknowledgement, the ACK number refers to:
options: A sequential number beginning with 1, incremented per exchange
The sequence number of the last packet successfully received
The number of the packet expected next
A number negotiated at session establishment
questions.explanation: The TCP ACK number is 'expectational' — it identifies the NEXT byte the receiver expects. If segments 1-3 arrived correctly, the ACK says '4' — meaning: received through 3, send me 4 next.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-08 Q76; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Which information does 'show cdp neighbors detail' reveal? (Choose the correct set)
options: Router Platform, IOS version, IP address only
Router Platform, all Layer 3 addresses (one per protocol), IOS version, ports, Router Name
Router Name, IP address and mask, incoming port only
questions.explanation: 'show cdp neighbors detail' reveals: device ID (router name), all Layer 3 addresses (one per protocol — not secondary addresses), platform, IOS version, incoming/outgoing ports, and holdtime. 'show cdp neighbors' (without detail) shows less.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q8; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 34: CDP and LLDP

questions.question: What is the class and major network for IP address 191.12.45.254?
questions.explanation: First octet 191 falls in 128–191 → Class B. Class B default mask is 255.255.0.0, so the major network is 191.12.0.0. The .45.254 portion is the subnet/host part.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q9; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: What statement about the IP address 0.10.10.10 is most correct?
options: This is a Class A address
This is a reserved private address
For a Class A address, this indicates 'this route'
This is not a legal address
questions.explanation: No address can have a value of 0 in the first octet — IOS will reject it with 'Not a valid host address'. Class A is 1–126 (excluding 127 which is loopback). 0.x.x.x is unassignable.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q10; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: What is the maximum number of hosts a Class C address supports using its default subnet mask?
questions.explanation: Class C default mask is /24 (255.255.255.0) leaving 8 host bits. Formula: 2^8 - 2 = 254 hosts. Subtract 2 for: network address (all host bits=0) and broadcast (all host bits=1).
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q11; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: The IP address classes that a network administrator would most likely assign to a host are:
options: Class A, B, C, and D
Class A, B, C, D, and E
Class A, B, and C only
questions.explanation: Only Classes A, B, and C are unicast and assignable to hosts. Class D (224–239) is reserved for multicast groups. Class E (240–255) is reserved for experimental use.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-09 Q12; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: The way TCP uses acknowledgements is best referred to as:
questions.explanation: TCP uses 'expectational acknowledgements': the ACK number specifies the next byte expected, not the last received. If segments 12, 13, 14 arrived, the ACK is 15 — the receiver expects segment 15 next.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q10; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Which of the following protocols most likely use UDP? (Choose the correct group)
questions.explanation: UDP is used by: DNS (port 53), TFTP (port 69), SNMP (port 161), NFS, DHCP, VoIP, streaming. TCP is used by: FTP (20/21), Telnet (23), SMTP (25), HTTP (80), HTTPS (443).
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q11; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 3

questions.question: The protocol IP is best described as:
options: Determines the data link address for known MAC addresses
Determines network addresses when data link addresses are known
Provides connectionless, best-effort delivery routing of datagrams
Provides connection-oriented, reliable delivery of datagrams
questions.explanation: IP (Internet Protocol) is connectionless (no session setup) and best-effort (no guarantee of delivery, ordering, or error recovery). Reliability is provided by upper-layer TCP if required.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q12; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: The protocol ICMP is best described as:
options: Helps IP by determining data link addresses for known IP addresses
Resolves MAC addresses to IP addresses
Resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses
Helps IP by providing control and messaging capabilities
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) provides error messages and diagnostics for IP: Destination Unreachable, Source Quench, Echo (ping), Echo Reply (pong), Time Exceeded (traceroute). ICMP messages are carried inside IP datagrams.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q13; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: What two protocols work together to send a 'host unreachable' message to a source host?
questions.explanation: An ICMP 'Destination Unreachable' message is generated and then encapsulated inside an IP datagram to be routed back to the source. ICMP cannot function without IP — every ICMP message is carried in an IP packet.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-10 Q14; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Which of the following does NOT define a purpose of the OSI Reference Model?
options: Define standard interfaces for plug-and-play and multi-vendor integration
Constrain or define the implementation of network functions
Allow areas to evolve without changes in one area impacting others
Make it easier to understand the complexity of internetworking
questions.explanation: The OSI model defines INTERFACES between layers, not how the functions inside a layer are implemented. Implementation is left to vendors. The model promotes interoperability, modularity, and understanding — but does NOT dictate code.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-11 Q1; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 1

questions.question: The OSI Presentation layer is primarily responsible for: (Choose two — select the best combined answer)
options: Reliable end-to-end communication using connection-oriented protocols
Code formatting and conversion (e.g., EBCDIC to ASCII) and managing encryption
Coordinating applications like NFS and SQL across different hosts
Segmentation and reassembly of upper-layer application data
questions.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation) handles: data format translation (EBCDIC↔ASCII, JPEG, MPEG), compression, and encryption/decryption. Layer 5 (Session) handles application coordination. Layer 4 (Transport) handles segmentation/reassembly.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-11 Q2; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 1

questions.question: In the context of multiplexing and demultiplexing, a Port Number is best described as:
options: A method ensuring data arrives in the correct encapsulation format
Assembly of data from different OSI layers into separate data streams
A method of distinguishing data from different applications over a single data stream
The numbering system for identifying outgoing or incoming data segments
questions.explanation: Port numbers are used at Layer 4 to multiplex multiple application flows over a single IP address. The combination of IP address + port number = socket. The port identifies which application/process the data belongs to.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-11 Q3; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: Handshaking is a technique found primarily at which layer of the OSI model?
options: Data Link layer during Token Ring framing
Network layer if a connection-oriented protocol is used
Transport layer when TCP is being used
questions.explanation: TCP's three-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) occurs at Layer 4 (Transport). The Session layer (Layer 5) manages session establishment logically, but the actual handshake mechanism is TCP at Layer 4.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-11 Q4; Jeremy's IT Lab - CCNA Course, Day 3

questions.question: Establishing a TCP window during session setup allows for: (Choose the best combined answer)
options: Multiple frame types to be sent over the same media type
Multiple data segments to be sent before the sender requires an ACK, and more efficient bandwidth use
Separate data streams from different hosts to a common host
Guaranteed in-order delivery without sequence numbers
questions.explanation: TCP windowing (flow control) allows the sender to transmit multiple segments before requiring an acknowledgement — filling the 'pipe' and maximizing throughput. The window size dynamically adjusts based on network conditions.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com 98-11 Q5; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 3

questions.question: What does NVRAM store on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: NVRAM (Non-Volatile RAM) stores the startup-config, which persists across reboots. RAM stores the running-config (lost on reboot). Flash stores the IOS image. ROM stores POST and bootstrap code.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q1; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 8

questions.question: You run 'show interfaces serial0' and see 'Serial0 is up, line protocol is down'. What does this indicate?
options: The cable is unplugged at the physical layer
The serial line has a Layer 2 problem — likely no keepalives or encapsulation mismatch
The interface is administratively shut down
The IP address is incorrectly configured
questions.explanation: 'Up/Down' means: Physical (Layer 1) is connected but Data Link (Layer 2) protocol has failed. Causes: no keepalives, encapsulation mismatch (HDLC vs PPP), or no clock rate on DCE. 'Administratively down' means 'shutdown' was applied.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q18; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 8

questions.question: Which WAN components do NOT belong to the customer?
questions.explanation: CO (Central Office) = the telco's facility/switch; Demarc (Demarcation point) = where provider responsibility ends and customer responsibility begins. Both belong to the carrier. CPE (Customer Premises Equipment) and DTE belong to the customer.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q10; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 2 Ch 15

questions.question: A router interface is described as 10.10.1.1/23. What is its subnet mask?
questions.explanation: /23 = 23 bits set to 1. First 16 bits cover first two octets (255.255). Next 7 bits set: 11111110 = 254. Last octet = 0. So mask = 255.255.254.0. This subnet spans two /24 blocks: 10.10.0.0–10.10.1.255.
questions.source_ref: CCNAPrep.com CCNA 640-407 Practice Q12; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1 Ch 7

questions.question: Which of the following is a benefit of the OSI layered model?
options: It constrains vendors to use specific implementations
Changes to one layer do not affect other layers (modular modification)
It reduces the number of protocols needed
It eliminates the need for standardized interfaces
questions.explanation: A key benefit of the OSI model is modular modification — changes to one layer don't require changes to others. It clarifies general function rather than specific implementation, and actually increases interoperability through standardized interfaces.
questions.source_ref: Talbert/Lammle CCNA Guide; Cisco ICRC course

questions.question: What is the name of the PDU (Protocol Data Unit) at the Transport layer?
questions.explanation: The Transport layer PDU is called a Segment. Network layer = Packet/Datagram, Data Link = Frame, Physical = Bits.
questions.source_ref: Talbert CCNA Guide talbert2.htm; CCNA_help.htm PDU table

questions.question: At which OSI layer is data encapsulated into FRAMES?
questions.explanation: Data Link Layer (Layer 2) encapsulates data into frames. The Network Layer adds a header to create Packets, the Transport layer creates Segments, and the Physical layer converts frames to bits.
questions.source_ref: Talbert CCNA Data Encapsulation guide; OSI Reference Model

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for data compression, encryption, and format translation?
questions.explanation: The Presentation Layer (Layer 6) handles data formatting, compression, encryption/decryption, and translation between data formats (ASCII, EBCDIC, JPEG, MPEG, etc.).

questions.question: Which OSI layer establishes, manages, and terminates communication sessions between applications?
questions.explanation: The Session Layer (Layer 5) establishes, manages, and terminates sessions. Examples include NFS, SQL, RPC, X Windows, NetBIOS, ZIP (Zone Information Protocol). Token management occurs here.

questions.question: The Data Link layer is subdivided into two sublayers. What are they?
options: LLC (Logical Link Control) and MAC (Media Access Control)
questions.explanation: IEEE subdivided the Data Link layer into LLC (Logical Link Control) — which manages communications between devices over a single link — and MAC (Media Access Control) — which manages protocol access to the physical medium and defines MAC addresses.

questions.question: What type of addressing does the Network Layer (Layer 3) use?
questions.explanation: The Network Layer uses logical addressing (IP addresses) for routing. The Data Link Layer uses physical (MAC) addressing. Logical addresses are hierarchical and can be assigned manually or dynamically.

questions.question: What is the correct order of the 5 steps of data encapsulation (top to bottom)?
questions.explanation: The 5 encapsulation steps are: (1) User info → Data, (2) Data → Segments (Transport header added), (3) Segments → Packets (Network header with logical addresses), (4) Packets → Frames (Data Link header for physical medium), (5) Frames → Bits (electrical signal on physical medium).

questions.question: What is the first octet range for Class A IP addresses?
questions.explanation: Class A addresses range from 1 to 127 in the first octet (127.0.0.0 is reserved for loopback). Class A uses Net.Node.Node.Node format, supporting 127 networks and approximately 16 million hosts per network.
questions.source_ref: CCNA_Cramsession.htm IP Address Classes table; 98-09 Q&A archive

questions.question: Which IP address class uses the first two octets for the network portion and supports approximately 65,534 hosts per subnet?
questions.explanation: Class B (128–191 first octet) uses Net.Net.Node.Node format. It supports ~16,384 networks and ~65,534 hosts per network (2^16 - 2 = 65,534). A first octet of 191 indicates Class B.

questions.question: How many usable host addresses does a Class C network provide?
questions.explanation: A Class C network has 8 host bits (2^8 = 256 addresses). Subtract 2 (network address and broadcast) = 254 usable host addresses. Class C range is 192–223 in the first octet.

questions.question: Using the subnetting formula, if you borrow 4 bits from the host portion of a Class C address, how many usable subnets do you get?
questions.explanation: Max subnets = 2^(borrowed bits) - 2 = 2^4 - 2 = 14. Usable hosts per subnet = 2^(remaining host bits) - 2 = 2^4 - 2 = 14. Note: Modern IOS no longer requires subtracting 2 for subnets, but classic CCNA formula subtracts 2.

questions.question: What is the well-known UDP port number used by TFTP (Trivial File Transfer Protocol)?
questions.explanation: TFTP uses UDP port 69. Other key ports: FTP data=20, FTP control=21, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, BootP server=67, BootP client=68, TFTP=69, HTTP=80, SNMP=161. TFTP/SNMP/DNS/NFS use UDP; Telnet/FTP/HTTP use TCP.

questions.question: What is the standard Cisco encapsulation method for IP packets on Ethernet interfaces?
questions.explanation: ARPA (also called Ethernet_II) is the default encapsulation for IP on Cisco Ethernet interfaces. SNAP is the default for Token Ring. NOVELL-ETHER is for IPX on Ethernet. SAP is Ethernet_802.2 LLC. The 'show interfaces' output shows 'Encapsulation ARPA'.

questions.question: What is the correct IOS command to configure an IP address on a router interface (from the Router(config-if)# prompt)?
questions.explanation: The correct command is 'ip address {IP address} {subnet mask}' under interface configuration mode. The mask is required. Shorthand in CIDR notation (/24) is also accepted in modern IOS. Always use 'no shutdown' after to bring the interface up.

questions.question: Which commands display the ARP cache on a Cisco router? (Choose two that apply)
questions.explanation: Both 'show ip arp' and 'show arp' display the ARP cache, which maps IP addresses to MAC addresses. The ARP table shows: Protocol, Address, Age, Hardware Address, Type, and Interface. To clear it, use 'clear arp-cache'.

questions.question: RARP (Reverse Address Resolution Protocol) is used for which purpose?
options: Resolving an IP address to a MAC address
Resolving a destination IP address by sending a broadcast
Resolving an IP address when only the MAC address is known (used by diskless workstations)
Mapping a hostname to an IP address
questions.explanation: RARP allows a host to discover its own IP address when it only knows its MAC address. A diskless workstation broadcasts its MAC address, and a RARP server responds with an assigned IP. ARP does the opposite: maps IP to MAC. RARP is largely replaced by DHCP today.

questions.question: Which of the following applications use TCP (connection-oriented transport)? Choose two.
questions.explanation: Telnet (port 23) and FTP (ports 20/21) use TCP because they require reliable, ordered delivery. TFTP (port 69) and SNMP (port 161) use UDP because they prioritize speed over reliability. DNS (port 53) uses both TCP and UDP.

questions.question: Which command clears the IP ARP cache on a Cisco router?
questions.explanation: The command 'clear arp-cache' clears all dynamic ARP entries from the router's ARP table. This is useful after changing an IP address or NIC card on a host to force the router to re-learn the new MAC-to-IP mapping.

questions.question: When do collisions occur in an Ethernet network?
options: When multiple packets are placed on a serial link
When multiple stations listen for traffic and transmit at the same time
When the network is segmented by bridges
When a router forwards a broadcast
questions.explanation: Collisions occur in Ethernet (CSMA/CD) when two or more stations sense no traffic and begin transmitting simultaneously. The first station to detect the collision sends a jamming signal, then all stations back off for a random timer before retransmitting. Collisions do NOT occur on serial links (point-to-point) or Token Ring (token passing).

questions.question: How many usable subnets does a Class C network have with subnet mask 255.255.255.252?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.252 borrows 6 bits from the host portion (252 = 11111100 in binary). Subnets = 2^6 - 2 = 64 - 2 = 62. Each subnet has only 2 usable hosts (2 host bits, 2^2 - 2 = 2). This is commonly used for point-to-point serial links.

questions.question: On an IP over Ethernet network, when Host A sends data to Host B, what type of address is used for actual frame delivery?
questions.explanation: At the Data Link layer, actual frame delivery uses physical (MAC) addresses. Even though IP addresses are used for routing between networks, within a single network segment the Ethernet frame must carry source and destination MAC addresses. ARP resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses for this purpose.

questions.question: What does ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) do?
options: Maps a MAC address to an IP address
Maps an IP address to a MAC address
Maps a hostname to an IP address
Maps a MAC address to a port number
questions.explanation: ARP maps a known IP address to an unknown MAC address. When Host A needs to send a frame to Host B (whose IP is known from routing), it broadcasts an ARP request asking 'Who has IP x.x.x.x?' Host B replies with its MAC address. The mapping is cached in the ARP table.

questions.question: What are the primary benefits of IP subnetting?
options: Conserves IP addresses and creates a hierarchical addressing scheme
Eliminates the need for routing protocols
Reduces the number of routers required
questions.explanation: IP subnetting provides two main benefits: (1) Conservation of IP address space by dividing large networks into smaller subnets, and (2) Creation of a hierarchical addressing structure which enables efficient routing and summarization (CIDR).

questions.question: Given IP address 193.243.12.43 and subnet mask 255.255.255.128, what is the subnet address?
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.128 = /25 — the last octet divides at bit 128. Host octet is 43. 43 < 128, so this is in the zero subnet (193.243.12.0/25). Subnet boundaries: 193.243.12.0 (network) through 193.243.12.127 (broadcast). The host 193.243.12.43 falls in this range. Note: The answer 'none of the above' from the original archive was misleading; 193.243.12.0 is correct.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) operate?
questions.explanation: ARP bridges Layer 2 (Data Link - MAC addresses) and Layer 3 (Network - IP addresses). It is considered a Layer 2 protocol in operation but uses Layer 3 addresses. ARP sends a Layer 2 broadcast containing a Layer 3 IP address to discover the MAC address of a host.

questions.question: Which OSI layer sends and receives binary (bit-level) information over physical media?
questions.explanation: The Physical Layer (Layer 1) transmits and receives raw bits as electrical, optical, or radio signals. It defines voltage levels, timing, cable specifications, and connectors. It does not understand frames or packets — it simply moves 1s and 0s.

questions.question: Which OSI layer provides end-to-end (source-to-destination) connections between hosts?
questions.explanation: The Transport Layer (Layer 4) provides end-to-end logical communication between hosts. TCP provides reliable, connection-oriented service; UDP provides unreliable, connectionless service. The Network Layer routes between networks but doesn't provide end-to-end reliability.

questions.question: Which OSI layer controls access to the physical media (defines when devices can transmit)?
questions.explanation: The Data Link Layer (Layer 2), specifically the MAC (Media Access Control) sublayer, controls access to the physical medium. CSMA/CD (Ethernet) and Token Passing are MAC-layer mechanisms. The Physical Layer just converts bits to signals; it doesn't control who transmits when.

questions.question: Which OSI layer defines network addressing and determines the best path through the network?
questions.explanation: The Network Layer (Layer 3) uses logical addressing (IP addresses) to route packets through the internetwork. Routing protocols (OSPF, RIP, EIGRP) and IP itself operate here. Routers are Layer 3 devices.

questions.question: Which of the following are features of a connection-oriented transport service (TCP)? Choose all that apply.
questions.explanation: TCP (connection-oriented) provides: three-way handshake (SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK) to establish connections, flow control (windowing), error detection (checksum), error recovery (retransmission), and sequencing. UDP (connectionless) provides none of these — it just sends datagrams.

questions.question: In a TCP connection, when Host A receives a segment with SEQ number 100, what ACK value does Host A send back?
options: The ACK number from the previous exchange
questions.explanation: TCP uses 'expectational acknowledgements' — the ACK number means 'I received up to X, now send me X+1.' If you receive SEQ=100 (for a 1-byte packet), you ACK with 101, meaning 'I got 100, send 101 next.' This confirms receipt and requests the next byte.

questions.question: What UDP port number does SNMP (Simple Network Management Protocol) use?
questions.explanation: SNMP uses UDP port 161 for queries/gets (SNMP manager queries SNMP agent) and UDP port 162 for traps (SNMP agent sends unsolicited alerts to manager). SNMP uses UDP because management queries are simple, and lost queries are retried by the management system.

questions.question: In the TCP three-way handshake, the FIRST SYN packet has an ACK value of:
questions.explanation: The initial SYN packet has ACK = 0 because no data has been received yet to acknowledge. The sequence is: (1) Client → SYN (SEQ=x, ACK=0), (2) Server → SYN-ACK (SEQ=y, ACK=x+1), (3) Client → ACK (SEQ=x+1, ACK=y+1). The 'established' ACL keyword blocks packets with ACK=0 (initial SYN).

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TTL (Time To Live) field in an IP packet?
options: To measure the transit time for the packet
To prevent IP packets caught in routing loops from traversing the network forever
To set the packet's priority for QoS
questions.explanation: TTL is a counter decremented by 1 at each router hop. When TTL reaches 0, the router discards the packet and sends an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message back to the source. This prevents packets from circulating endlessly in routing loops. Default TTL is typically 64 or 128 (OS-dependent), maximum is 255.

questions.question: What subnet mask would you use for a Class C network to support exactly 62 hosts per subnet?
questions.explanation: You need at least 62 hosts per subnet. 2^n - 2 ≥ 62. Try n=6: 2^6 - 2 = 62 ✓. Six host bits remain, so 2 bits are used for subnetting. Last octet mask = 11000000 = 192. Full mask: 255.255.255.192. Each subnet gives 2^6 - 2 = 62 usable hosts and 2^2 - 2 = 2 usable subnets (or 4 with ip subnet-zero).

questions.question: What is the subnet address for host 172.16.35.123/20?
questions.explanation: /20 mask = 255.255.240.0. Third octet subnet blocks: every 16 (256-240=16). 35 ÷ 16 = 2 remainder 3. Subnet starts at 2×16 = 32. So subnet = 172.16.32.0/20. Host range: 172.16.32.1 – 172.16.47.254. Broadcast: 172.16.47.255. Host 35.123 falls in this range ✓.
questions.source_ref: CCNA subnetting practice; Jeremy's IT Lab Day 14-15

questions.question: A /30 subnet is most commonly used for which type of connection?
options: Large enterprise LAN with hundreds of hosts
Point-to-point serial links between routers (only 2 hosts needed)
questions.explanation: /30 = 255.255.255.252. Host bits = 2. Usable hosts = 2^2 - 2 = 2. Perfect for point-to-point links connecting two routers — one IP per router end. Using a /30 conserves IP address space compared to assigning a /24 to a 2-device link. Alternatively, /31 or unnumbered interfaces can also be used for P2P links.

questions.question: Which of the following are private IP address ranges (RFC 1918)?
questions.explanation: RFC 1918 defines three private address ranges not routable on the Internet: 10.0.0.0/8 (Class A), 172.16.0.0/12 (Class B, range 172.16.0.0–172.31.255.255), and 192.168.0.0/16 (Class C). These require NAT to communicate with public Internet hosts. 127.0.0.0/8 is loopback; 169.254.0.0/16 is APIPA (link-local).
questions.source_ref: Jeremy's IT Lab CCNA Day 44: NAT/Private IPs; Wendell Odom OCG Vol 1

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for the network 192.168.10.0/24?
questions.explanation: For 192.168.10.0/24: all host bits set to 1 = 192.168.10.255 (broadcast). Network address = 192.168.10.0 (all host bits 0). First usable host = 192.168.10.1. Last usable host = 192.168.10.254. Total usable hosts = 2^8 - 2 = 254.

questions.question: Which Data Link sublayer gives upper layers independence from the LAN media access method?
questions.explanation: The LLC (Logical Link Control) sublayer sits above MAC and provides a common interface to upper layers, shielding them from the specifics of the underlying LAN technology (Ethernet, Token Ring, etc.).

questions.question: A MAC address is 48 bits long and composed of two parts. What are those two parts?
questions.explanation: The first 24 bits are the Organizationally Unique Identifier (OUI) assigned by IEEE to the vendor. The last 24 bits are a serial number assigned by the vendor to make each card unique. Example: Cisco OUI = 00:00:0C.

questions.question: What is the major difference between an Ethernet II frame and an IEEE 802.3 frame?
options: 802.3 uses a two-byte Length field where Ethernet II uses a Type field
Ethernet II uses a two-byte sequence number where 802.3 uses a Type field
Ethernet II uses a SNAP header to describe type
questions.explanation: Ethernet II (DIX Ethernet) uses a 2-byte Type field to indicate the upper-layer protocol. IEEE 802.3 replaced this with a 2-byte Length field; type information is carried in the 802.2 LLC header (SSAP/DSAP) or SNAP extension.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the 802.3 SNAP (Subnet Access Protocol) format?
options: To identify the length of the frame
questions.explanation: SNAP is an extension of the 802.2 LLC header (SSAP=AA, DSAP=AA, Control=03) that includes a 5-byte SNAP header: 3-byte OUI + 2-byte protocol type. This allows identification of proprietary protocols (e.g., Cisco CDP) within 802.3 frames.

questions.question: What does the TCP Window Size field establish?
options: The maximum number of bytes in a single frame
The allowable bandwidth for the TCP session
The number of 32-bit words in the TCP header
The number of octets the sender is willing to accept
questions.explanation: The Window Size field in a TCP segment specifies how many octets the sender of the segment is willing to receive (its receive buffer size). This enables flow control — the receiver tells the sender how much data it can buffer.

questions.question: A Telnet session to a remote host is addressed to which port number?
questions.explanation: Telnet uses TCP port 23. This is a well-known port: the server listens on TCP/23, and the Telnet client connects to it. Note that Telnet is cleartext — SSH (TCP/22) is preferred for secure remote management.

questions.question: What is the IP address class and major network for the address 191.12.45.254?
questions.explanation: First octet 191 falls in the range 128-191, making this a Class B address. Class B addresses use the first two octets for the network, so the major network is 191.12.0.0 with a default mask of 255.255.0.0.

questions.question: What is most correct about the IP address 0.10.10.10?
options: It is a valid Class A address
It is a reserved private address
It is not a legal IP address
It can only be used on private networks
questions.explanation: No IP address may have a value of 0 in the first octet. Cisco IOS will reject it: 'Not a valid host address - 0.10.10.10'. The valid unicast range starts at 1.x.x.x (Class A starts at 1, not 0).

questions.question: What is the maximum number of hosts a Class C address can support with its default subnet mask?
questions.explanation: A Class C address has 8 host bits (fourth octet). Using the formula 2^N - 2 = 2^8 - 2 = 256 - 2 = 254 hosts. We subtract 2 for the network address (all host bits = 0) and broadcast address (all host bits = 1).

questions.question: Which IP address classes would an administrator most likely assign to a host?
options: Class A, B, C, and D
Class A, B, C, D, and E
questions.explanation: Only Class A (1-126), B (128-191), and C (192-223) addresses are assigned to hosts. Class D (224-239) is reserved for multicast groups. Class E (240-255) is reserved for experimental use. Neither D nor E can be assigned to a host.

questions.question: TCP uses 'expectational' acknowledgements. If the sender sent segments 12, 13, and 14 and all were received correctly, what does the receiver ACK with?
questions.explanation: TCP uses expectational acknowledgements: the ACK number indicates the NEXT expected segment, not the last received. After correctly receiving segments 12, 13, and 14, the receiver sends ACK=15, meaning 'I got 12-14, send me 15 next.'

questions.question: Which four of the following protocols most commonly use UDP? (Choose the option listing all four.)
questions.explanation: UDP is used by: DNS (port 53), TFTP (port 69), SNMP (port 161/162), and NFS. FTP (TCP 20/21), Telnet (TCP 23), and SMTP (TCP 25) all use TCP because they require reliable delivery.

questions.question: The IP protocol is best described as:
options: Determines data link addresses for known MAC addresses
Provides connection-oriented, reliable delivery of datagrams
Provides connectionless, best-effort delivery routing of datagrams
questions.explanation: IP is a connectionless, best-effort, unreliable protocol — it tries to deliver packets but does not guarantee delivery, ordering, or error recovery. Reliability is added by upper-layer protocols like TCP. IP simply routes datagrams toward the destination.

questions.question: ICMP is best described as:
options: Determines data link addresses for known IP addresses
Resolves MAC addresses to IP addresses
Adds reliable connection-oriented service to IP
Helps IP by providing control and messaging capabilities
questions.explanation: ICMP (Internet Control Message Protocol) is a helper protocol for IP. It provides error reporting and diagnostic functions such as Destination Unreachable, Time Exceeded, Echo Request/Reply (ping), and Source Quench. ICMP messages are carried inside IP datagrams.

questions.question: Two protocols are needed to send a 'host unreachable' message back to a source host. Which two?
questions.explanation: ICMP generates the 'Destination Unreachable' message, but ICMP messages are carried inside IP datagrams. So both IP (to route the message) and ICMP (to generate the control message) are needed to send a host unreachable notification.

questions.question: The OSI Presentation layer is primarily responsible for which two functions?
options: Code formatting/conversion (e.g. EBCDIC to ASCII) and encryption
Managing NFS and SQL application sessions
Segmentation and reassembly of application data
questions.explanation: Layer 6 (Presentation) handles data formatting: it translates between character sets (e.g., EBCDIC ↔ ASCII), manages data compression, and handles encryption/decryption. Sessions are managed at Layer 5 (Session); segmentation is Layer 4 (Transport).

questions.question: In the context of multiplexing/demultiplexing, a port number is best described as:
options: A method of ensuring correct encapsulation format for the receiving host
A numbering system that identifies data portions of segments
A method of distinguishing data from different applications on a single data stream
Assembly of data from different OSI layers into separate streams
questions.explanation: Port numbers allow the Transport layer to multiplex multiple application conversations over a single network connection. Each port number identifies a specific application, enabling the receiving host to demultiplex incoming data to the correct application.

questions.question: TCP handshaking (the 3-way handshake) occurs at which OSI layer?
options: Data-link layer during Token Ring framing
Network layer when using connection-oriented protocols
Transport layer when TCP is being used
questions.explanation: The TCP 3-way handshake (SYN → SYN-ACK → ACK) is a Layer 4 (Transport) function. TCP is a connection-oriented Transport layer protocol. Layer 5 (Session) manages broader application sessions, but the handshake itself is TCP/Transport.

questions.question: TCP windowing allows for which two benefits?
options: Multiple frame types over one media type, and separate data streams
Multiple segments sent before the sender requires an ACK, and more efficient bandwidth use
Multiple segments sent before the receiver requires an ACK, and separate data streams
Guaranteed delivery and in-order packet processing
questions.explanation: TCP windowing allows the sender to transmit multiple segments before waiting for an acknowledgement (controlled by window size). This keeps the pipe full and makes much more efficient use of available bandwidth compared to stop-and-wait.

questions.question: For a Class C network, how many subnets and hosts per subnet does a /27 mask (255.255.255.224) provide?
questions.explanation: /27 borrows 3 bits from the host portion of a Class C address. Subnets = 2^3 - 2 = 6. Remaining host bits = 5, so hosts = 2^5 - 2 = 30. Block size = 256 - 224 = 32. Subnet boundaries: .0, .32, .64, .96, .128, .160, .192, .224.

questions.question: For a Class C network, what mask gives 14 subnets with 14 hosts each?
questions.explanation: /28 (255.255.255.240) borrows 4 bits. Subnets = 2^4 - 2 = 14. Host bits = 4, hosts = 2^4 - 2 = 14. Block size = 256 - 240 = 16. This is a common exam pairing: 14 subnets AND 14 hosts with /28.

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for a /30 prefix (commonly used for point-to-point links)?
questions.explanation: /30 = 255.255.255.252. It provides only 2 usable host addresses (2^2 - 2 = 2), making it ideal for point-to-point WAN links. The block size is 4. Class C example: 62 subnets with 2 hosts each.

questions.question: What are the three basic methods of flow control used in networking?
options: Buffering, source quench messages, and windowing
CRC checking, framing, and sequence numbers
questions.explanation: The three flow control methods: (1) Buffering — temporarily stores excess data in memory; can overflow. (2) Source quench messages — receiver sends ICMP source quench to tell sender to slow down. (3) Windowing — sender must get ACK after sending N bytes before sending more; dynamically adjusts based on network conditions.

questions.question: For communication throughout an internetwork, which TWO things are required? (Choose the answer listing both.)
options: A hierarchical naming server (DNS) and a host file
A two-part addressing scheme (Network/Host) and a routable protocol (IP or IPX)
TCP protocol and a two-part address scheme
questions.explanation: Internetworking requires: (1) Two-part addressing (Network portion + Host portion) so routers can identify which network to forward to. (2) A routable protocol (IP, IPX) — protocols like NetBEUI or LAT have no Layer 3 addressing and cannot be routed. Note: TCP is a Transport layer protocol, not a routed protocol.

questions.question: What are the three layers of the Cisco hierarchical network design model?
questions.explanation: Cisco's three-layer hierarchical model: Access Layer — connects end devices (PCs, phones, printers) to the network; provides port security, VLANs, PoE. Distribution Layer — aggregates access layer switches; enforces routing policies, ACLs, inter-VLAN routing; redundancy. Core Layer — high-speed backbone connecting distribution layers; optimized for speed, minimal policy processing. Collapsed core = distribution + core combined.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does a router operate?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network). They make forwarding decisions based on IP addresses (logical addresses). Switches operate at Layer 2 (MAC addresses). Hubs at Layer 1 (physical signal). Firewalls can operate at Layers 3-7.

questions.question: What is the correct top-to-bottom order of the OSI model layers?
options: Application, Presentation, Session, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical
Physical, Data Link, Network, Transport, Session, Presentation, Application
Application, Transport, Network, Data Link, Physical, Session, Presentation
Application, Session, Presentation, Network, Transport, Data Link, Physical
questions.explanation: OSI layers top-to-bottom: 7-Application, 6-Presentation, 5-Session, 4-Transport, 3-Network, 2-Data Link, 1-Physical. Mnemonic top-to-bottom: 'All People Seem To Need Data Processing.' Bottom-to-top: 'Please Do Not Throw Sausage Pizza Away.'

questions.question: What is the correct name for the PDU (Protocol Data Unit) at the Transport layer?
questions.explanation: PDU names by layer: Layer 7-5 = Data, Layer 4 (Transport) = Segment, Layer 3 (Network) = Packet, Layer 2 (Data Link) = Frame, Layer 1 (Physical) = Bit. TCP uses Segments; UDP also produces Segments (or Datagrams). IP produces Packets. Ethernet produces Frames.

questions.question: When would you use a crossover cable to connect two devices directly?
questions.explanation: Crossover cables connect like devices: switch-to-switch, hub-to-hub, PC-to-PC, router-to-router (same type). Straight-through cables connect unlike devices: PC-to-switch, switch-to-router. Modern devices with Auto-MDI/MDIX can auto-detect and adjust, making the cable type less critical. Fiber uses SC/LC/ST connectors.

questions.question: A host has IP 192.168.10.65 with subnet mask 255.255.255.192. What is the network address?
questions.explanation: /26 = 255.255.255.192. Block size = 256 - 192 = 64. Subnets: .0, .64, .128, .192. Host .65 falls in the .64 subnet. Network address = 192.168.10.64, Broadcast = 192.168.10.127, Usable range = .65 to .126.

questions.question: How many usable host addresses does a /25 subnet provide?
questions.explanation: /25 = 255.255.255.128. 2^7 = 128 total addresses. Usable hosts = 128 - 2 = 126 (subtract network address and broadcast). Block size = 256 - 128 = 128. Compare: /24 = 254 hosts, /25 = 126, /26 = 62, /27 = 30, /28 = 14, /29 = 6, /30 = 2.

questions.question: You need to subnet 10.0.0.0/8 to support 500 hosts per subnet. Which subnet mask should you use?
questions.explanation: /23 = 2^9 - 2 = 510 usable hosts (sufficient for 500). /24 = 2^8 - 2 = 254 hosts (too few). /22 = 2^10 - 2 = 1022 hosts (works but wastes addresses). Choose the smallest block that still accommodates the requirement: /23 is optimal.

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for the subnet 172.16.4.0/22?
How many subnets and hosts per subnet does 172.16.0.0/20 provide?
questions.explanation: 172.16.0.0 is a Class B network (/16). Borrowing 4 bits gives /20. Subnets = 2^4 = 16. Hosts per subnet = 2^12 - 2 = 4094. Block size in third octet = 16. Subnets: 172.16.0.0, 172.16.16.0, 172.16.32.0... up to 172.16.240.0.

questions.question: What is VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)?
options: Using the same subnet mask for all subnets in a network
Assigning different subnet masks to different subnets to use address space efficiently
Varying the subnet mask based on traffic load
A technique for aggregating routes using summarization
questions.explanation: VLSM allows different subnets within the same network to have different prefix lengths, matching the actual number of hosts needed. For example: a WAN link gets /30 (2 hosts), a server subnet gets /26 (62 hosts), an office gets /24 (254 hosts). VLSM requires classless routing protocols (RIPv2, EIGRP, OSPF, BGP).

questions.question: Which of the following is a characteristic of UDP but NOT TCP?
questions.explanation: UDP is connectionless — no 3-way handshake, no acknowledgments, no sequencing, no flow control. This makes UDP fast with minimal overhead. TCP has all of those features (connection setup, ACKs, sequencing, windowing). UDP is used for DNS, DHCP, TFTP, VoIP, video streaming where speed > reliability.

questions.question: What is the correct sequence of the TCP three-way handshake?
questions.explanation: TCP connection establishment: 1) Client sends SYN (synchronize), 2) Server responds with SYN-ACK (synchronize-acknowledge), 3) Client sends ACK (acknowledge). Connection is established. For termination: FIN → FIN-ACK → FIN → FIN-ACK (4-way). The handshake sets initial sequence numbers for both directions.

questions.question: Match these protocols to their correct port numbers: FTP, SSH, Telnet, SMTP
questions.explanation: Key port numbers: FTP data=20, FTP control=21, SSH=22, Telnet=23, SMTP=25, DNS=53, HTTP=80, HTTPS=443, POP3=110, IMAP=143, SNMP=161/162, RDP=3389. Also: DHCP server=67, DHCP client=68, TFTP=69, NTP=123, Syslog=514.

questions.question: Which port does HTTPS use?
questions.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP Secure) uses TCP port 443. HTTP uses TCP port 80. Port 8080 is commonly used as an alternative HTTP port. HTTPS uses TLS/SSL to encrypt web traffic. When configuring ACLs, remember: `eq www` = port 80, `eq 443` for HTTPS.

questions.question: What type of network topology connects each device to every other device directly?
questions.explanation: Full mesh: every device connects directly to every other device. n devices require n(n-1)/2 connections. Highly redundant but expensive. Partial mesh reduces connections while keeping some redundancy. Star topology: all devices connect to a central hub/switch. Bus: all on a single cable. Ring: daisy-chained in a circle.

questions.question: How do switches and routers affect collision and broadcast domains?
options: Switches break both collision and broadcast domains; routers do neither
Switches break collision domains per port; routers break both collision and broadcast domains
Both switches and routers break broadcast domains
Hubs break collision domains; switches break broadcast domains
questions.explanation: Each switch port is its own collision domain (dedicated full-duplex). Switches do NOT break broadcast domains by default — broadcasts flood all ports in the same VLAN. Routers break both collision AND broadcast domains — they do not forward broadcasts between interfaces. VLANs create separate broadcast domains on a switch.

questions.question: Which IP address ranges are defined as private (RFC 1918)?
questions.explanation: RFC 1918 private IP ranges: 10.0.0.0 – 10.255.255.255 (/8), 172.16.0.0 – 172.31.255.255 (/12), 192.168.0.0 – 192.168.255.255 (/16). These are not routable on the Internet. Also: 169.254.0.0/16 is APIPA (automatic private IP), and 127.0.0.0/8 is loopback.

questions.question: What does CSMA/CD stand for and what problem does it solve?
options: Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection — manages access on shared Ethernet
Channel Select Multiple Access / Channel Differentiation — manages VLAN channels
Carrier Sense Message Access / Collision Data — manages multicast traffic
Centralized Switch Management Architecture / Carrier Detection — manages switch uplinks
questions.explanation: CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access with Collision Detection): devices listen before transmitting (carrier sense), multiple devices can attempt transmission (multiple access), devices detect collisions and use random backoff before retrying (collision detection). Used in half-duplex Ethernet. Full-duplex Ethernet eliminates collisions entirely, making CSMA/CD unnecessary.

questions.question: What is the minimum and maximum size of an Ethernet II frame (without 802.1Q tag)?
questions.explanation: Ethernet II frame: minimum 64 bytes (to ensure collision detection works on 100m segments), maximum 1518 bytes (14-byte header + 1500-byte payload/MTU + 4-byte FCS). Frames under 64 bytes are 'runts' (padded or errored). Frames over 1518 are 'giants' (unless jumbo frames are enabled, up to 9000 bytes). With 802.1Q tag: max = 1522 bytes.

questions.question: What is the difference between LAN, WAN, and MAN?
options: LAN=local area (building/campus), MAN=metropolitan area (city), WAN=wide area (large geographic area)
questions.explanation: Network types by geography: LAN (Local Area Network) = covers a building or campus, typically owned by one organization. MAN (Metropolitan Area Network) = covers a city or metropolitan area, often using fiber. WAN (Wide Area Network) = covers large geographic areas (countries, global), often leased from a service provider. SAN = Storage Area Network (data center storage).

questions.question: How does the TCP/IP (DOD) model map to the OSI model?
options: They are identical models with different names
questions.explanation: TCP/IP (DOD) model has 4 layers vs OSI's 7: Application (OSI 7-5: Application, Presentation, Session), Transport (OSI 4: Transport), Internet (OSI 3: Network), Network Access (OSI 2-1: Data Link, Physical). Protocols: Application=HTTP/FTP/DNS/SMTP, Transport=TCP/UDP, Internet=IP/ICMP/ARP, Network Access=Ethernet/802.11.

questions.question: What is the purpose of ARP (Address Resolution Protocol)?
options: Translates domain names to IP addresses
Resolves IP addresses to MAC addresses on a local network segment
Assigns IP addresses to hosts dynamically
Routes packets between different IP networks
questions.explanation: ARP resolves a known IP address to its corresponding MAC address on a local segment. When a host wants to send a frame to a local IP, it broadcasts an ARP Request ('Who has 192.168.1.1?'), and the target replies with its MAC address (ARP Reply). The result is cached in the ARP table for future use. IPv6 uses NDP instead of ARP.

questions.question: What is 127.0.0.1 used for?
options: IPv4 loopback address (localhost) — used to test local TCP/IP stack
Broadcast address for the local subnet
questions.explanation: 127.0.0.1 (and the entire 127.0.0.0/8 range) is the IPv4 loopback address. Packets sent to 127.x.x.x are processed by the local TCP/IP stack without leaving the device. Used to test if the TCP/IP stack is functioning. In IPv6, ::1 is the loopback. `ping 127.0.0.1` tests local stack; `ping <own IP>` tests the NIC.

questions.question: What is the main difference between single-mode and multimode fiber optic cable?
options: Single-mode uses copper core; multimode uses glass core
Single-mode supports longer distances with laser light; multimode uses LED light for shorter distances
Single-mode is cheaper; multimode is more expensive
Single-mode supports multiple wavelengths; multimode supports only one
questions.explanation: Single-mode fiber (SMF): narrow core (~9 μm), uses laser, supports very long distances (up to 100 km+), used for WAN/backbone. Multimode fiber (MMF): wider core (50/62.5 μm), uses LED or VCSEL laser, cheaper but shorter distances (up to ~550 m for 10 GbE). SMF has a smaller core, so only one light mode propagates — less dispersion, longer reach.

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for /29?
questions.explanation: /29 = 8 bits in first 3 octets + 5 bits in last octet (29-24=5 borrowed from host). The last octet = 11111000 = 248. Mask = 255.255.255.248. Block size = 256 - 248 = 8. Hosts = 2^3 - 2 = 6 usable. Compare: /28=.240 (14 hosts), /29=.248 (6 hosts), /30=.252 (2 hosts).

questions.question: What is the structure of a MAC address?
options: 48 bits: randomly assigned by the OS
questions.explanation: MAC addresses are 48 bits (6 bytes), written as 12 hex digits (e.g., 00:1A:2B:3C:4D:5E). First 24 bits = OUI (Organizationally Unique Identifier, assigned to the manufacturer by IEEE). Last 24 bits = device-specific serial assigned by the manufacturer. The 7th bit (U/L) indicates universal/local. The 8th bit (I/G) indicates unicast/multicast.

questions.question: What causes a duplex mismatch on an Ethernet link?
options: One end uses TCP; the other uses UDP
One end is configured for half-duplex and the other for full-duplex
One end uses Fast Ethernet and the other uses Gigabit Ethernet
One end uses 802.1Q tagging and the other doesn't
questions.explanation: A duplex mismatch occurs when one interface is full-duplex and the other is half-duplex. The full-duplex side transmits continuously while the half-duplex side detects this as collisions and backs off. Symptoms: high collisions/errors, poor throughput. Auto-negotiation resolves this automatically when both sides support it. Mismatches often occur when one side is manually set and the other is set to auto.

questions.question: What connector type is used with standard Cat5e/Cat6 UTP Ethernet cables?
questions.explanation: RJ-45 (Registered Jack 45) is the 8-pin connector used for Ethernet UTP/STP cables (Cat5, Cat5e, Cat6). RJ-11 is the 6-pin connector used for telephone lines. BNC was used with older 10BASE2 (ThinNet) coaxial Ethernet. SC and LC are fiber optic connectors.

questions.question: What is the TTL (Time to Live) field in the IP header used for?
options: Specifies how long a packet can be cached by DNS
Limits the lifespan of a packet by being decremented by each router; packet dropped when TTL reaches 0
Sets the maximum transmission unit for the packet
Identifies the IP version (4 or 6) of the packet
questions.explanation: TTL prevents packets from looping forever in the network. Each router decrements TTL by 1 before forwarding. When TTL reaches 0, the router discards the packet and sends an ICMP 'Time Exceeded' message back to the source. Default TTL: Windows = 128, Linux/macOS = 64, Cisco = 255. Traceroute exploits TTL to discover hops along the path.

questions.question: What is a directed broadcast address?
options: The all-ones broadcast 255.255.255.255 sent to all networks
The broadcast address for a specific subnet (all host bits = 1)
An IGMP multicast address for a specific group
A loopback address for directed testing
questions.explanation: A directed broadcast is the broadcast address for a specific subnet — all host bits set to 1. Example: for 192.168.1.0/24, the directed broadcast is 192.168.1.255. Contrast with limited broadcast (255.255.255.255) which is not routed. Directed broadcasts can be routed by routers (though Cisco disables forwarding by default with `no ip directed-broadcast`).

questions.question: How can you abbreviate the IPv6 address 2001:0DB8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001?
questions.explanation: IPv6 abbreviation rules: 1) Remove leading zeros in each 16-bit group (0DB8 → DB8, 0000 → 0). 2) Replace ONE sequence of consecutive all-zero groups with :: (can only be done once). Result: 2001:DB8::1. The :: can only appear once in an address. To expand, :: represents enough groups of zeros to make 8 groups total.

questions.question: In the 5-step data encapsulation process, what happens in step 2?
options: A transport header (segment) is added to the application data
A network header with IP addresses is added
The data is converted to bits on the wire
questions.explanation: 5-step encapsulation: 1) Application data created, 2) Transport header added → Segment (TCP/UDP adds source/dest ports, sequencing), 3) Network header added → Packet (IP adds source/dest IP), 4) Frame header added → Frame (Ethernet adds MAC addresses, FCS), 5) Converted to bits for physical transmission. De-encapsulation is the reverse at the receiver.

questions.question: What is the difference between CDP and LLDP?
options: CDP is open standard; LLDP is Cisco proprietary
CDP is Cisco proprietary; LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol) is the IEEE 802.1AB open standard
CDP runs at Layer 3; LLDP runs at Layer 2
CDP is used for routing; LLDP is used for switching
questions.explanation: CDP (Cisco Discovery Protocol) is Cisco proprietary — discovers Cisco neighbors only. LLDP (Link Layer Discovery Protocol, IEEE 802.1AB) is the vendor-neutral standard — works with multi-vendor environments. Both run at Layer 2, are sent every 30 seconds, and discover device info (hostname, IP, model, interface). Enable LLDP with `lldp run` (global) and `lldp transmit`/`lldp receive` per interface.

questions.question: In a hierarchical three-layer network design, what is the purpose of the distribution layer?
options: Provides high-speed backbone connectivity between data centers
Connects end users to the network
Aggregates access layer switches, enforces policies, and provides routing between VLANs
Provides Internet connectivity and WAN edge services
questions.explanation: Hierarchical design layers: Access = end devices connect (PCs, phones, printers, port security). Distribution = aggregates access switches, inter-VLAN routing (Layer 3), policy enforcement, QoS, ACLs, summarization. Core = high-speed backbone (Layer 3), connects distribution/data centers, optimized for speed not policy. Collapsed core = combines distribution + core for smaller networks.

questions.question: What is the first usable host address in the subnet 10.0.128.0/17?
questions.explanation: /17 in the second octet: 128+0 = .128.0 to .255.255. Network address = 10.0.128.0. First usable = 10.0.128.1. Last usable = 10.0.255.254. Broadcast = 10.0.255.255. Total hosts = 2^15 - 2 = 32766. Block size in second octet = 256 - 128 = 128.

questions.question: How many bits need to be borrowed from the host portion to create at least 14 subnets?
questions.explanation: Subnets = 2^n where n = borrowed bits. Need at least 14 subnets: 2^3 = 8 (not enough), 2^4 = 16 (sufficient). Borrow 4 bits. This reduces host bits by 4. Example: Class C /24 borrows 4 bits → /28. Each subnet: 2^4 - 2 = 14 usable hosts. Compare: /26 borrows 2 bits (4 subnets, 62 hosts), /27 borrows 3 (8 subnets, 30 hosts), /28 borrows 4 (16 subnets, 14 hosts).

questions.question: What is 10.1.0.0/22 in binary for the last 2 meaningful octets (3rd and 4th)?
options: 00000000 to 00000011 in the 3rd octet, any value in 4th
questions.explanation: /22 borrows 6 bits from the 3rd octet (16+6=22). The first 2 bits of the 3rd octet are network bits (00 = .0). The remaining 2 bits (value 0-3) plus all 8 bits of the 4th octet are host bits. Network = 10.1.0.0, Broadcast = 10.1.3.255, covering .0.x through .3.x. Block size = 4 in 3rd octet.

questions.question: What is the key functional difference between a hub and a switch?
options: Hubs operate at Layer 3; switches operate at Layer 2
Hubs forward all frames to all ports (shared collision domain); switches forward frames to specific ports using MAC addresses
Hubs support VLANs; switches do not
Hubs are faster than switches because they don't need to look up MAC addresses
questions.explanation: Hub: Layer 1 device, repeats every bit to all ports — all connected devices share one collision domain, bandwidth is shared. Switch: Layer 2 device, learns MAC addresses and forwards frames only to the destination port — each port is its own collision domain, dedicated bandwidth. Switches replaced hubs in virtually all modern networks.

questions.question: Why was IPv6 developed?
options: IPv4 was too slow for modern networks
IPv4's 32-bit address space was running out; IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses providing vastly more addresses
IPv4 had no security features; IPv6 added mandatory IPsec
IPv4 didn't support routing; IPv6 added routing capabilities
questions.explanation: The primary driver for IPv6 is address exhaustion. IPv4 has 2^32 (~4.3 billion) addresses — insufficient for global growth. IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses: 2^128 = ~340 undecillion addresses (essentially unlimited). IPv6 also adds: stateless autoconfiguration (SLAAC), simplified header, no broadcast (uses multicast), neighbor discovery (replaces ARP), and built-in support for mobility.

questions.question: What is the network address and broadcast address for 192.168.100.200/27?
Which protocol is used to automatically assign IP addresses to devices on a network?
questions.explanation: DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) automatically assigns IP addresses, subnet masks, default gateways, and DNS servers to clients. RARP (Reverse ARP) was an older method (legacy). ARP resolves IP to MAC. DNS resolves names to IP addresses. DHCP uses UDP ports 67 (server) and 68 (client).

questions.question: Which protocol translates domain names (like www.example.com) to IP addresses?
questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) is the 'phone book' of the Internet — translates human-readable domain names to IP addresses. DNS uses UDP port 53 for standard queries. Record types: A (IPv4), AAAA (IPv6), MX (mail), CNAME (alias), PTR (reverse lookup), NS (name server). DNS resolution: client → local resolver → root servers → TLD servers → authoritative DNS.

questions.question: What is FTP used for and what ports does it use?
options: File Transfer Protocol — TCP 20 (data) and TCP 21 (control)
questions.explanation: FTP (File Transfer Protocol) transfers files between systems using two TCP connections: port 21 for control (commands like USER, PASS, LIST) and port 20 for data transfer (active mode). FTP sends credentials in cleartext — use SFTP (SSH FTP, port 22) or FTPS (FTP over TLS, port 990) for security. TFTP (Trivial FTP) uses UDP 69 — simpler, no authentication.

questions.question: At which OSI layers does a multilayer (Layer 3) switch operate?
questions.explanation: A multilayer switch (Layer 3 switch) operates at both Layer 2 (switching/MAC) and Layer 3 (routing/IP). It performs inter-VLAN routing in hardware using ASICs (much faster than router-on-a-stick). Layer 2 switch = MAC only. Layer 3 switch = MAC + IP routing. Some advanced switches also operate at Layer 4 (port-based QoS) but are still called 'multilayer' or 'L3 switches'.

questions.question: What are the two most common frequency bands for Wi-Fi, and what is the key trade-off?
options: 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz — 2.4 GHz has longer range but fewer channels; 5 GHz has shorter range but more channels and less interference
1.8 GHz and 5 GHz — 1.8 GHz is for outdoor, 5 GHz for indoor
2.4 GHz and 6 GHz — 2.4 GHz is newer; 6 GHz is legacy
5 GHz and 10 GHz — no real difference
questions.explanation: 2.4 GHz: longer range (waves penetrate walls better), only 3 non-overlapping channels (1,6,11), more congested (Bluetooth, microwaves, neighbors). 5 GHz: shorter range, many non-overlapping channels (up to 24+), less interference, higher throughput. 6 GHz (Wi-Fi 6E): even more spectrum. Rule of thumb: 2.4 GHz = range/coverage; 5 GHz = speed/capacity.

questions.question: What is the primary function of the Session layer (Layer 5) of the OSI model?
options: Converts data formats and provides encryption
Establishes, manages, and terminates communication sessions between applications
Transports segments between hosts with flow control
questions.explanation: Session layer (L5): establishes, manages, and terminates communication sessions. Handles dialog control (who talks when), synchronization (checkpoints for recovery), and token management. Examples: NetBIOS, NFS, SQL, RPC, sockets. Presentation (L6): encryption, compression, data format conversion. Transport (L4): reliable delivery, flow control, error recovery.

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for logical addressing and routing?
questions.explanation: Network layer (L3): logical (IP) addressing, routing between networks, path determination. Routers operate here. Protocols: IP, ICMP, ARP (some say L2/L3), OSPF, EIGRP. Data Link (L2): physical (MAC) addressing, framing, local delivery within a segment. Transport (L4): end-to-end delivery (TCP/UDP). Physical (L1): bits/signals on the wire.

questions.question: What two sublayers does the IEEE divide the Data Link layer into?
options: LLC (Logical Link Control) and MAC (Media Access Control) sublayers
questions.explanation: IEEE splits Layer 2 into: LLC (Logical Link Control, 802.2) — upper sublayer, protocol-independent, provides interface to upper layers, multiplexing. MAC (Media Access Control) — lower sublayer, hardware-specific, manages access to the physical medium (CSMA/CD), hardware addressing. This separation allows the LLC to work the same regardless of the underlying physical technology.

questions.question: Which OSI layer functions include flow control, multiplexing, virtual circuit management, and error recovery?
questions.explanation: Transport layer (L4) functions: flow control (sliding window), multiplexing (multiple applications on one link via port numbers), virtual circuit management (TCP connection setup/teardown), error checking and recovery (retransmission). TCP implements all these. UDP provides multiplexing (ports) without reliability. The transport layer abstracts the network from upper layers.

questions.question: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for data compression, encryption, and character encoding format conversion (e.g., ASCII to EBCDIC)?
questions.explanation: Presentation layer (L6): ensures information from one system's application layer is readable by another system. Handles: data format standards (JPEG, GIF, MPEG), data compression, encryption/decryption (SSL/TLS encryption often attributed here), and character set conversion (ASCII, EBCDIC, Unicode). In practice, TCP/IP stack collapses L5-L7 into the Application layer.

questions.question: Which 802.11 amendment introduced MIMO (Multiple Input Multiple Output) and operates in both 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz?
questions.explanation: 802.11 standards: 802.11b (2.4 GHz, 11 Mbps), 802.11a (5 GHz, 54 Mbps), 802.11g (2.4 GHz, 54 Mbps), 802.11n/Wi-Fi 4 (both bands, up to 600 Mbps, MIMO), 802.11ac/Wi-Fi 5 (5 GHz only, up to ~3.5 Gbps, MU-MIMO), 802.11ax/Wi-Fi 6 (both 2.4/5 GHz, OFDMA, up to ~9.6 Gbps). 802.11n introduced MIMO (multiple antennas for spatial multiplexing).

questions.question: What is the maximum theoretical throughput of 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5)?
questions.explanation: 802.11ac (Wi-Fi 5): 5 GHz only, multi-user MIMO (MU-MIMO), up to 8 spatial streams, 160 MHz channels, 256-QAM. Wave 1: ~1.3 Gbps. Wave 2: ~3.5 Gbps. 802.11ax (Wi-Fi 6): 9.6 Gbps theoretical, OFDMA, BSS coloring, Target Wake Time (TWT) for IoT. 802.11n: 600 Mbps. 802.11a/g: 54 Mbps.

questions.question: Which field in the TCP header is used for flow control to tell the sender how much data the receiver can accept?
questions.explanation: The TCP window size field specifies how many bytes the receiver can accept before requiring an acknowledgment. This is TCP's sliding window flow control mechanism.

questions.question: What is the TCP three-way handshake sequence?
