options: The receiver advertises how many bytes it can accept; the sender limits in-flight data to that amount
The sender transmits one segment and waits for an ACK before sending the next
Both sides negotiate window size only during the 3-way handshake and it never changes
The window size is fixed at 65,535 bytes for all connections
questions.explanation: TCP's sliding window is dynamic. The receiver advertises its window in each ACK, allowing the sender to keep multiple segments in flight without waiting for individual ACKs.
hints: Window size is in every TCP header, not just SYN

questions.question: Host A (10.1.1.1/24) sends a packet to 10.1.2.1. Its default gateway is 10.1.1.254. What does Host A place in the destination MAC address of the Ethernet frame?
questions.explanation: The destination IP is in a different subnet. Host A cannot reach it directly, so it sends the frame to the default gateway's MAC. The IP destination remains 10.1.2.1.
hints: Layer 2 vs Layer 3 addressing — only Layer 3 carries end-to-end destination

questions.question: An Ethernet frame has a Type field value of 0x0800. What does this indicate?
options: The payload is an IPv4 packet
The payload is an ARP packet
The payload is an IPv6 packet
questions.explanation: EtherType 0x0800 = IPv4. 0x0806 = ARP, 0x86DD = IPv6, 0x8100 = 802.1Q.
hints: Memorize the common EtherType values

questions.question: What is the maximum transmission unit (MTU) of a standard Ethernet frame payload?
questions.explanation: Standard Ethernet MTU (payload) is 1500 bytes. 1518 is the full frame (1500+18 header/trailer). 1522 adds 802.1Q tag. 9000 is a jumbo frame.
hints: MTU refers to the payload, not the entire frame

questions.question: Which mechanism does a host use to determine if a destination IP is on the same subnet without knowing the destination's MAC address?
options: ARP — broadcasts a request; if destination is local it replies directly
ICMP redirect from the default gateway
Proxy ARP response from the router
questions.explanation: ARP is used to resolve IPs to MACs on the same subnet. If the destination is in the same subnet the host ARPs for its MAC directly. If remote it ARPs for the gateway.
hints: ARP only works within a subnet

questions.question: Which IPv4 address class originally defined the range 128.0.0.0 to 191.255.255.255?
hints: First octet 128-191 = Class B

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv4 DF (Don't Fragment) bit?
options: Tells routers not to fragment the packet; if fragmentation is needed the packet is dropped and an ICMP Unreachable is sent
Prevents the packet from being duplicated across multiple paths
Instructs routers to use the express forwarding path
Disables TTL decrement for that packet
questions.explanation: DF bit prevents fragmentation. If the packet is too large for a link MTU, the router drops it and sends ICMP Type 3 Code 4 (fragmentation needed, DF set). Path MTU Discovery uses this.
hints: DF = Don't Fragment; used in PMTU discovery

questions.question: A UDP datagram is sent to port 53. The receiving host has no application listening on port 53. What happens?
options: The host sends an ICMP Port Unreachable message
The host sends a TCP RST
The datagram is queued until an application opens port 53
questions.explanation: When UDP delivers to a closed port, the kernel sends ICMP Type 3 Code 3 (Port Unreachable). TCP would send a RST, but this is UDP.
hints: ICMP Port Unreachable is for UDP; RST is for TCP

questions.question: Which layer of the OSI model is responsible for reliable delivery, segmentation, and flow control?
questions.explanation: The Transport layer (L4) provides reliable delivery (TCP), segmentation, and flow control. Network (L3) handles routing; Data Link (L2) handles framing.
hints: TCP and UDP live at Layer 4

questions.question: What is the key difference between a collision domain and a broadcast domain?
options: A collision domain is bounded by switches/bridges at L2; a broadcast domain is bounded by routers at L3
A collision domain is bounded by routers; a broadcast domain is bounded by switches
They are the same concept with different names
Switches extend both collision and broadcast domains
questions.explanation: Each switch port is its own collision domain (full-duplex eliminates collisions). A broadcast domain is bounded by routers or L3 devices — all ports in a VLAN share a broadcast domain.
hints: Switches break collision domains; routers break broadcast domains

questions.question: You have the block 192.168.10.0/24. You need 6 subnets each supporting at least 25 hosts. Which mask works?
questions.explanation: /27 = 32 addresses, 30 usable hosts, 8 subnets from a /24. Meets both requirements (≥25 hosts, ≥6 subnets). /28 only has 14 usable; /26 only gives 4 subnets.
hints: Check both host count and subnet count simultaneously

questions.question: An IP packet has the IP precedence bits set to 5. In DSCP terms, what is the equivalent PHB?
questions.explanation: IP Precedence 5 maps to CS5. CS values use the top 3 bits of the DSCP field: CS5 = 101000 binary = 40 decimal.
hints: CS (Class Selector) PHBs maintain backward compatibility with IP Precedence

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TCP FIN flag compared to RST?
options: FIN initiates a graceful four-way teardown allowing in-flight data to finish; RST abruptly terminates without draining
FIN abruptly resets the connection; RST allows graceful close
They perform identical functions; RST is the older term
FIN is used only by servers; RST only by clients
questions.explanation: FIN = graceful close (half-close, waits for remaining data). RST = abrupt termination, discards buffered data, used for errors or port-closed responses.
hints: FIN drains; RST drops

questions.question: Which two ICMP message types are used by the traceroute utility to map network paths? (Choose two)
questions.explanation: Traceroute sends probes with incrementing TTL values. Intermediate routers return ICMP Time Exceeded (Type 11) when TTL hits 0. The final hop returns ICMP Port Unreachable (Type 3 Code 3 for UDP) or Echo Reply for ICMP-based traceroute. Type 11 and Type 3 are the key responses.
hints: TTL expiry → Time Exceeded; destination reached → Port Unreachable or Echo Reply

questions.question: You perform a `show ip arp` and see an incomplete entry. What does this mean?
options: An ARP request was sent but no reply was received within the timeout period
The entry has expired and will be refreshed on next use
The MAC address is statically configured but the IP is unknown
The entry is being replicated to a standby router
questions.explanation: An 'Incomplete' ARP entry means the router sent an ARP request for that IP but received no reply — the host may be down or unreachable.
hints: Incomplete = sent ARP, no MAC received back

questions.question: Which mechanism allows a single IPv4 address to represent an entire organization on the public internet?
questions.explanation: NAT translates private RFC 1918 addresses to a public IP. PAT (a form of NAT) multiplexes many private addresses to one public IP using port numbers.
hints: Think about how home routers work

questions.question: What is the primary difference between TCP and UDP regarding connection establishment?
options: TCP performs a 3-way handshake before data transfer; UDP sends data immediately without setup
TCP sends data immediately; UDP requires 3-way handshake
Both require handshake but UDP uses a 2-way handshake
Neither requires handshake in modern IP stacks
questions.explanation: TCP is connection-oriented — SYN, SYN-ACK, ACK before data. UDP is connectionless — no handshake, data is sent immediately at the cost of reliability.
hints: TCP = reliable + ordered; UDP = fast + unordered

questions.question: Which is the correct IPv4 private address range for the 172.x.x.x block?
questions.explanation: RFC 1918 defines 172.16.0.0/12 = 172.16.0.0 through 172.31.255.255. This is a /12 block (12 fixed bits).
hints: 172 private = /12 block, not /8 or /16

questions.question: A switch receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC. What action does it take?
options: Floods the frame out all ports except the one it arrived on
Drops the frame and sends an error to the source
Sends the frame to the default gateway
Forwards the frame only to the root bridge
questions.explanation: Unknown unicast flooding — when the destination MAC is not in the MAC address table, the switch floods the frame out all ports in the same VLAN except the ingress port.
hints: Unknown unicast = flood; known unicast = forward

questions.question: What is the result of performing a logical AND of 10.50.200.130 with the mask 255.255.255.192?
hints: AND each octet: 130 AND 192 = ?

questions.question: Which statement about CSMA/CD is true in a modern switched network?
options: CSMA/CD is effectively unused because full-duplex links eliminate collisions
CSMA/CD is used on all switch ports to detect collisions
CSMA/CD only applies to wireless networks
Modern switches use CSMA/CA instead of CSMA/CD
questions.explanation: Full-duplex Ethernet eliminates collisions entirely. CSMA/CD is defined in the standard but not exercised in modern switched, full-duplex environments.
hints: Full-duplex = separate TX/RX paths = no collisions

questions.question: Which summarization correctly represents the networks 192.168.4.0/24, 192.168.5.0/24, 192.168.6.0/24, and 192.168.7.0/24?
questions.explanation: 4 contiguous /24s starting at .4. Block size 4 → 2^2 → borrow 2 bits → /22. Network: 192.168.4.0/22 covers .4.0–.7.255.
hints: Find the block size (4 subnets), compute prefix: 24-2=22

questions.question: What is the binary representation of the decimal 192?
questions.explanation: 192 = 128+64 = 11000000 in binary. This is also the first byte of Class C addresses and the mask value for /26.

questions.question: A router receives a packet with TTL=1. What action does it take?
options: Drops the packet and sends ICMP Time Exceeded to the source
Decrements TTL to 0 and forwards the packet
Forwards the packet without decrementing TTL
Buffers the packet and requests a route update
questions.explanation: The router decrements TTL from 1 to 0, drops the packet, and sends ICMP Type 11 (Time Exceeded) to the original source IP.
hints: TTL=1 means this router is the last hop — decrement to 0 = drop + ICMP

questions.question: What is the broadcast address for the subnet containing host 10.10.32.50/20?
questions.explanation: /20 = 255.255.240.0, block size 16 in 3rd octet. 32/16=2 (exact), so network=10.10.32.0, broadcast=10.10.47.255.
hints: Block size for /20 in 3rd octet: 256-240=16; 32 is a multiple of 16

questions.question: Which field in the TCP header guarantees ordered delivery of segments?
questions.explanation: TCP sequence numbers allow the receiver to reorder segments that arrive out of sequence. Each byte of data is numbered; the receiver reassembles in order.
hints: Sequence numbers enable ordering; ACK numbers confirm receipt

questions.question: A host receives a DHCP offer with IP 192.168.1.50, mask 255.255.255.0, gateway 192.168.1.1, lease 86400 seconds. When does the host first attempt to renew the lease?
questions.explanation: DHCP renewal (T1) occurs at 50% of the lease time. The host sends a unicast DHCP Request to the server. At 87.5% (T2) it broadcasts to any server if the unicast renewal failed.
hints: T1=50%, T2=87.5% of lease

questions.question: What is the total number of usable host addresses in a /19 network?
questions.explanation: /19 = 13 host bits. 2^13 - 2 = 8192 - 2 = 8190 usable hosts.
hints: Host bits = 32-19=13; usable = 2^13-2

questions.question: A host at 172.20.100.1/22 wants to communicate with 172.20.102.100/22. Which statement is true?
options: They are in the same /22 subnet and can communicate directly without a router
They are in different subnets and need a router
172.20.100.1 is a network address and cannot be a host
questions.explanation: /22 = 255.255.252.0, block size 4 in 3rd octet. 100 / 4 = 25 (exact), network = 172.20.100.0, broadcast = 172.20.103.255. Both 172.20.100.1 and 172.20.102.100 are in this range.
hints: Block size for /22: 256-252=4; 100 is divisible by 4

questions.question: Which protocol uses port 443 and provides encrypted web traffic?
questions.explanation: HTTPS (HTTP over TLS/SSL) uses TCP port 443. HTTP uses 80. SFTP uses 22. SNMP uses UDP 161/162. LDAP uses 389.
hints: 443 = HTTPS, 80 = HTTP

questions.question: How many subnets can be created from a /24 network using a /27 mask?
questions.explanation: /27 borrows 3 bits from a /24 (27-24=3). 2^3 = 8 subnets.
hints: Subnets = 2^(new bits borrowed) = 2^(27-24)

questions.question: Which RFC defines the IPv4 loopback address range?
questions.explanation: 127.0.0.0/8 is the loopback range (127.0.0.1 is most commonly used). Defined in RFC 5735 (special-use IPv4 addresses).
hints: 127.x.x.x = loopback range, not just 127.0.0.1

questions.question: At which OSI layer does a router primarily make forwarding decisions?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network), forwarding packets based on destination IP addresses and routing tables.
hints: Routers = Layer 3; switches = Layer 2

questions.question: What happens during TCP's TIME_WAIT state after a connection close?
options: The socket waits 2×MSL before fully closing to handle late duplicate segments
The client retransmits all unacknowledged data
The server reclaims the port immediately
The session is kept open for keep-alive messages
questions.explanation: TIME_WAIT (typically 2×MSL = 60-240 seconds) ensures all late duplicate segments expire before the socket is reused. This prevents a new connection from receiving stale segments.
hints: TIME_WAIT prevents stale segments from confusing new connections on same port

questions.question: Which addresses are in the APIPA range used by Windows when DHCP fails?
questions.explanation: APIPA (Automatic Private IP Addressing) uses 169.254.0.0/16. Windows hosts auto-assign an address from this range when DHCP fails, per RFC 3927.
hints: 169.254.x.x = APIPA / link-local

questions.question: A /30 subnet is commonly used for what purpose?
options: Point-to-point WAN links — provides exactly 2 usable host addresses
Connecting 4 hosts on a LAN segment
questions.explanation: /30 provides 2 usable host addresses (2^2-2=2), ideal for point-to-point links between two routers, minimizing IP waste.
hints: 2 usable addresses = exactly what a point-to-point link needs

questions.question: What is the decimal value of the 8-bit binary number 11110000?
questions.explanation: 11110000 = 128+64+32+16 = 240. This is the mask value for /28 in the subnet octet (256-16=240 where /28 leaves 4 host bits).

questions.question: Which protocol resolves a known IP address to a MAC address on the same subnet?
questions.explanation: ARP (Address Resolution Protocol) resolves IP to MAC. RARP (Reverse ARP) is obsolete — it resolved MAC to IP for diskless workstations.
hints: ARP: IP → MAC; DNS: name → IP

questions.question: In the OSI model, which two adjacent layers communicate via Service Access Points (SAPs)?
options: Each layer uses SAPs to offer services to the layer above and consume services from the layer below
SAPs are used only at the physical layer
SAPs are Cisco proprietary and not part of the OSI model
questions.explanation: SAPs are the defined interfaces between OSI layers. Each layer provides services to the layer above through its SAP and requests services from the layer below.
hints: SAPs = inter-layer interfaces in the OSI model

questions.question: What is the purpose of the Differentiated Services Code Point (DSCP) field in IPv4?
options: Marks packets for QoS treatment (priority, bandwidth guarantees) by network devices
Identifies the transport protocol encapsulated in the IP payload
questions.explanation: DSCP (6 bits of the ToS/DS byte) marks packets with PHBs (Per-Hop Behaviors) like EF (voice), AF (business), or BE (best effort). Routers use these marks to apply QoS policies.
hints: DSCP = QoS marking; EF=voice, AF=assured forwarding, BE=default

questions.question: Which Layer 4 protocol is used by VoIP RTP streams, and why?
options: UDP — low overhead and latency tolerate occasional loss; ordering is handled by RTP sequence numbers
TCP — reliability ensures no voice packets are lost
Both TCP and UDP — RTP negotiates dynamically
SCTP — provides both reliability and multi-streaming
questions.explanation: RTP (Real-time Transport Protocol) uses UDP. VoIP tolerates small loss better than retransmission delay. RTP has its own sequence numbers and timestamps for jitter compensation.
hints: Voice=UDP; retransmitting old voice packets makes it worse, not better

questions.question: How does a managed switch learn MAC addresses?
options: By inspecting the source MAC address of incoming frames and associating it with the ingress port
By broadcasting an ARP request and recording replies
By reading MAC addresses from the IP routing table
By receiving MAC update frames from the root bridge
questions.explanation: MAC learning: switches examine the SOURCE MAC and ingress port of each arriving frame, adding the mapping to the MAC address table (CAM table).
hints: Source MAC + ingress port = learned entry

questions.question: What is the default aging time for a dynamic MAC address table entry on a Cisco switch?
questions.explanation: Cisco switch default MAC table aging time is 300 seconds (5 minutes). If no frames are received from a device within that time, the entry is removed.
hints: Default MAC aging = 300 seconds

questions.question: What is the purpose of ICMP Type 3 Code 1 (Host Unreachable)?
options: The router knows the destination network but cannot reach the specific host (ARP failure or host down)
The destination network does not exist in the routing table
The destination port on the host has no application listening
The packet's DF bit is set but fragmentation is needed
questions.explanation: ICMP Type 3: Destination Unreachable. Code 1 = Host Unreachable (router knows the subnet but the host is down or ARP fails). Code 0 = Network Unreachable, Code 3 = Port Unreachable.
hints: Code 0=network, Code 1=host, Code 3=port, Code 4=frag needed

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a valid reason to use VLSM (Variable Length Subnet Masking)?
options: To ensure all subnets use the same mask for simplified management
To allocate different-sized subnets to match actual host requirements
To conserve IP address space by sizing subnets appropriately
To accommodate point-to-point links with /30 masks alongside /24 LANs
questions.explanation: VLSM allows different mask lengths for different subnets. Using the same mask everywhere is classful fixed-length subnetting, the opposite of VLSM.
hints: VLSM = variable = different masks; same mask = fixed-length

questions.question: Two routers are directly connected via a serial link. The link uses 192.0.2.0/30. Which addresses are assigned to each router?
questions.explanation: /30 network: 192.0.2.0 (network), 192.0.2.1 and 192.0.2.2 are usable hosts, 192.0.2.3 is broadcast.
hints: Network=.0, Hosts=.1 and .2, Broadcast=.3

questions.question: A host at 10.0.0.0/8 needs to reach 10.255.255.255. What type of address is 10.255.255.255?
options: The directed broadcast of the 10.0.0.0/8 network
A valid host address in the 10.0.0.0/8 network
questions.explanation: 10.255.255.255 is the directed broadcast address of 10.0.0.0/8 (all host bits set to 1). Routers can forward directed broadcasts; local broadcasts (255.255.255.255) are not routed.
hints: Directed broadcast = network ID with all host bits = 1

questions.question: TCP uses which port number range for dynamically assigned ephemeral (client) ports?
questions.explanation: IANA defines ephemeral ports as 49152–65535. Linux commonly uses 32768–60999. Well-known ports are 0–1023. The CCNA typically references the IANA ephemeral range.
hints: Well-known=0-1023; registered=1024-49151; ephemeral=49152-65535

questions.question: Which IPv4 address is reserved for all-hosts multicast (ping the entire local subnet's multicast-aware devices)?
questions.explanation: 224.0.0.1 is the All Hosts group (all multicast-capable hosts on the local subnet). 224.0.0.2 = all routers. 224.0.0.5 = all OSPF routers.
hints: 224.0.0.1 = all hosts; 224.0.0.2 = all routers

questions.question: A router has two paths to 192.168.1.0/24: one via OSPF (metric 110, cost 20) and one via EIGRP (metric 90, cost 1000). Which path is installed in the routing table?
options: The directly connected path always wins
questions.explanation: Administrative distance is compared BEFORE metric. EIGRP AD=90, OSPF AD=110. EIGRP wins regardless of metric value. Metrics are only compared between routes from the SAME source.
hints: AD comparison: lower AD wins; metrics only compared within same protocol

questions.question: What distinguishes a hub from a Layer 2 switch in terms of collision domains?
options: A hub has one collision domain for all ports; a switch gives each port its own collision domain
A switch has one collision domain; a hub has one per port
They are identical in collision domain handling
A hub has no collision domains; a switch has one per broadcast domain
questions.explanation: Hubs repeat signals to all ports — all devices share one collision domain. Switches segment each port into its own collision domain (and operate full-duplex, eliminating collisions entirely).
hints: Hub = 1 collision domain; switch = 1 per port

questions.question: What is the minimum Ethernet frame size, and what happens when a frame is smaller?
options: 64 bytes minimum; frames smaller than 64 bytes are called runts and discarded
32 bytes minimum; smaller frames are padded
128 bytes minimum; smaller frames are held in buffer
questions.explanation: Minimum Ethernet frame (excluding preamble) is 64 bytes (14B header + 46B min payload + 4B FCS). Frames shorter than 64 bytes are runts and are discarded by switches.
hints: Minimum frame = 64 bytes; smaller = runt = discard

questions.question: Which ICMP message type does `ping` use for its echo request?
questions.explanation: Ping sends ICMP Type 8 (Echo Request) and expects ICMP Type 0 (Echo Reply). Type 3 = Destination Unreachable, Type 11 = Time Exceeded, Type 5 = Redirect.
hints: ping: sends Type 8, receives Type 0

questions.question: How many bits are in an IPv6 address?
questions.explanation: IPv6 addresses are 128 bits, written as eight 16-bit groups in hexadecimal separated by colons (e.g., 2001:0db8:85a3::8a2e:0370:7334).
hints: IPv6 = 128 bits = 8 groups of 16 bits (hextets)

questions.question: What does the IPv6 double colon (::) notation represent?
options: One or more consecutive groups of all-zero 16-bit hextets, compressed for readability
A separator between the network prefix and the interface ID
A wildcard mask in IPv6 ACLs
The zone ID separator for link-local addresses
questions.explanation: :: compresses one or more consecutive all-zero groups. It can appear only ONCE in an address. 2001:db8::1 expands to 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001.
hints: :: = consecutive zeros compressed; can only appear once per address

questions.question: Which OSI layer does SSL/TLS operate at?
options: Session/Presentation (Layer 5/6) — typically considered Layer 6 (Presentation) in strict OSI, or between L4-L7 in practice
questions.explanation: TLS/SSL is often placed at the Presentation layer (L6) or Session layer (L5) in the OSI model, as it provides encryption and session management. In TCP/IP it's considered part of the application stack.
hints: TLS = encryption = Presentation layer (OSI L6) by most exam frameworks

questions.question: Which mechanism prevents a TCP sender from overwhelming a slow receiver?
options: Flow control via the receive window in TCP headers
Congestion control via ICMP Source Quench
questions.explanation: TCP flow control: the receiver advertises its remaining buffer space (receive window) in each ACK. The sender cannot transmit beyond what the window allows, preventing receiver overflow.
hints: Flow control = receive window; congestion control = cwnd (sender-side)

questions.question: What is the purpose of TCP's slow start algorithm?
options: Begins with a small congestion window (cwnd) that doubles each RTT, probing for available bandwidth gradually to avoid overwhelming the network
Slows transmission when the receiver window drops below 1 KB
Limits throughput to the configured CIR on a WAN link
Throttles the sender if ICMP Source Quench messages are received
questions.explanation: Slow start: cwnd starts at 1 MSS and doubles each RTT (exponential growth) until a loss event or the slow-start threshold (ssthresh). Prevents TCP from flooding the network at startup.
hints: Slow start: exponential growth of cwnd from 1 MSS

questions.question: What is the correct expanded form of the IPv6 address 2001:db8::1?
questions.explanation: :: replaces the longest run of consecutive all-zero groups. 2001:db8 then :: = 5 groups of zeros then :0001. Padded: 2001:0db8:0000:0000:0000:0000:0000:0001.
hints: Expand each group to 4 hex digits; :: fills zero groups

questions.question: What is the EUI-64 process used for in IPv6?
options: Derives a 64-bit interface identifier from the 48-bit MAC address by inserting FFFE in the middle and inverting the 7th bit
Assigns a /64 prefix to each host automatically
Generates a random IID to protect privacy
Converts IPv4 addresses to IPv6 for tunneling
questions.explanation: EUI-64: take the 48-bit MAC, split in half, insert FF:FE in the middle (making 64 bits), then invert bit 7 (U/L bit). Used for SLAAC to generate the interface ID portion of the IPv6 address.
hints: MAC → split at midpoint → insert FF:FE → invert bit 7 = EUI-64 IID

questions.question: A packet is addressed to 239.10.0.1. What type of address is this?
questions.explanation: 239.x.x.x is within the 224.0.0.0/4 multicast range. Specifically, 239.0.0.0/8 is the administratively scoped multicast range (private multicast, similar to RFC 1918).
hints: 224-239.x.x.x = multicast; 239.x.x.x = admin-scoped (private)

questions.question: What does 'full duplex' mean for an Ethernet link?
options: The device can transmit and receive simultaneously on separate wire pairs, eliminating collisions
The device alternates between transmitting and receiving, avoiding collisions using CSMA/CD
The link has twice the normal bandwidth
Both ends negotiate speed and duplex automatically
questions.explanation: Full duplex: TX and RX use separate wire pairs, so both endpoints transmit simultaneously without collisions. Half duplex: shared medium, one direction at a time, CSMA/CD required.
hints: Full duplex = simultaneous TX and RX = no collisions

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv4 header Identification field?
options: Groups fragments of a fragmented datagram so the receiver can reassemble them
Identifies the protocol in the payload (TCP, UDP, ICMP)
questions.explanation: The Identification field holds a unique value for each datagram. All fragments of the same original datagram share the same Identification value, allowing the receiver to reassemble them.
hints: Identification + Flags + Fragment Offset = fragmentation/reassembly mechanism

questions.question: What RFC defines Classless Inter-Domain Routing (CIDR) aggregation of IPv4 addresses?
questions.explanation: CIDR was originally defined in RFC 1518/1519 (1993) and later updated by RFC 4632. It replaced classful addressing and enabled route summarization.
hints: CIDR allows prefix lengths other than 8/16/24; RFC 4632 is current

questions.question: A /26 mask divides a /24 into how many subnets, and how many usable hosts per subnet?
questions.explanation: /26 borrows 2 bits from /24 (2^2=4 subnets). Host bits=6, usable=2^6-2=62 per subnet.
hints: 4 subnets × 62 hosts; block size = 64

questions.question: Which PDU name is used at the Data Link layer?
questions.explanation: OSI PDU names: L1=Bits, L2=Frame, L3=Packet, L4=Segment (TCP) or Datagram (UDP), L5-7=Data.
hints: L2=Frame, L3=Packet, L4=Segment

questions.question: What is the address 255.255.255.255 used for in networking?
options: Limited broadcast — sent to all hosts on the local network segment; not forwarded by routers
Directed broadcast — sent to all hosts in a specific remote subnet
Loopback address for testing the IP stack
questions.explanation: 255.255.255.255 is the limited broadcast, sent to all devices on the local subnet. Routers do not forward this. Directed broadcasts (e.g., 192.168.1.255 for /24) CAN be forwarded.
hints: 255.255.255.255 = limited broadcast (local only); directed broadcast = all-1s in host portion

questions.question: Which transport protocol provides multiplexing but NOT guaranteed delivery or ordering?
questions.explanation: UDP provides port-based multiplexing (and optional checksum) but no connection establishment, no guaranteed delivery, and no ordering. TCP provides all of those.
hints: UDP: fast, simple, no reliability; TCP: reliable, ordered, connection-oriented

questions.question: What does a router do when its routing table has no matching entry for a destination and no default route?
options: Drops the packet and sends ICMP Destination Unreachable (Network Unreachable) to the source
Sends the packet to the nearest hub
Floods the packet to all interfaces
Buffers the packet until a route is found
questions.explanation: When no route matches and no default route exists, the router drops the packet and sends ICMP Type 3 Code 0 (Network Unreachable) to the packet's source.
hints: No route = drop + ICMP Network Unreachable to source

questions.question: Which option correctly describes the IPv4 address 192.168.1.0?
options: Network address of the 192.168.1.0/24 subnet — not assignable to a host
questions.explanation: The address with all host bits = 0 is the network ID. 192.168.1.0 in a /24 subnet is the network address. The first usable host is 192.168.1.1.
hints: All host bits 0 = network address; all host bits 1 = broadcast

questions.question: How does a switch handle a unicast frame when the destination MAC IS in the MAC address table?
options: Forwards the frame only to the specific port mapped to that MAC address
Floods the frame to all ports in the VLAN
Sends the frame to the default gateway
Discards the frame after logging the destination MAC
questions.explanation: Known unicast forwarding: if the destination MAC is in the MAC table, the switch forwards the frame ONLY out the port associated with that MAC. This is the fundamental efficiency advantage over hubs.
hints: Known unicast = targeted forward to one port; unknown unicast = flood

questions.question: How many Class C networks exist in the IPv4 address space?
questions.explanation: Class C: first octet range 192-223 = 32 values (5 bits). But the /24 host bits leave 8 host bits. Network bits in Class C: 21 bits for network = 2^21 = 2,097,152 Class C networks.
hints: Class C: 3 fixed octets + 1 host octet; 21 variable network bits

questions.question: In a /23 subnet, what is the maximum number of usable host addresses?
questions.explanation: /23 = 9 host bits. 2^9 - 2 = 512 - 2 = 510 usable hosts.
hints: Host bits = 32-23=9; usable = 2^9-2 = 510

questions.question: Which tool uses ICMP Echo Requests with incrementing TTL values to discover network path hops?
questions.explanation: traceroute (Linux/macOS) / tracert (Windows) sends probes with TTL=1, 2, 3, etc. Each router decrements TTL; at TTL=0 it sends ICMP Time Exceeded back, revealing each hop's IP.
hints: traceroute = TTL manipulation; each hop returns ICMP Time Exceeded

questions.question: What is the subnet mask for /21?
questions.explanation: /21 = 255.255.248.0. Third octet: 8 bits borrowed means top 5 bits set = 11111000 = 248.
hints: /21 leaves 11 host bits; third octet = 256-8=248

questions.question: A host has address 192.168.100.200/25. What is its network address?
questions.explanation: /25 = 255.255.255.128. Block size 128 in last octet. 200 is in the second block: 128-255. Network = 192.168.100.128, broadcast = 192.168.100.255.
hints: Block size for /25 = 128; 200 > 128 so network = .128

questions.question: Which well-known port does FTP use for data transfer (active mode)?
questions.explanation: FTP: port 21 = control channel (commands). Port 20 = data transfer in active mode. Passive mode uses a negotiated ephemeral port. SSH=22, Telnet=23.
hints: FTP: 21=control, 20=data (active mode)

questions.question: What does the TCP Urgent (URG) flag indicate?
options: The segment contains urgent data that should be processed immediately, bypassing normal buffering
The connection is being urgently reset
The sender is running low on buffer space
questions.explanation: TCP URG flag: marks urgent data that should be delivered to the application out-of-band, bypassing the normal data queue. The Urgent Pointer field indicates where urgent data ends.
hints: URG + Urgent Pointer = out-of-band data processing

questions.question: Which address does an IPv6 host send a Router Solicitation to?
questions.explanation: IPv6 NDP Router Solicitation (ICMPv6 type 133) is sent to FF02::2 (all routers multicast). Routers respond with Router Advertisements to FF02::1 (all nodes).
hints: RS to FF02::2 (all routers); RA from router to FF02::1

questions.question: What is the IPv6 prefix for link-local addresses and how is the IID typically derived?
options: FE80::/10 prefix; IID derived from EUI-64 or random (RFC 7217)
questions.explanation: Link-local: FE80::/10 prefix. Hosts form the 64-bit IID using EUI-64 (from MAC) or random (RFC 7217 for privacy). The full address is FE80::<IID>/64.
hints: FE80::/10 = link-local; EUI-64 or random IID

questions.question: A network uses 10.0.0.0/8. A junior engineer summarizes the entire range as a single route. What is the correct prefix notation?
questions.explanation: 10.0.0.0/8 is the entire Class A private block (10.0.0.0 through 10.255.255.255). The /8 prefix denotes 8 fixed network bits.
hints: 10.0.0.0/8 = entire 10.x.x.x private range

questions.question: Which layer of the TCP/IP model corresponds most closely to OSI Layers 5, 6, and 7?
questions.explanation: TCP/IP model: Application=OSI L5+L6+L7; Transport=OSI L4; Internet=OSI L3; Network Access=OSI L1+L2.
hints: TCP/IP Application = OSI Session+Presentation+Application (L5-L7)

questions.question: What happens to the TTL field in an IP packet when a router forwards it?
options: Decremented to 0 and the packet is dropped
questions.explanation: Each router decrements TTL by exactly 1. The router that receives a packet with TTL=1 decrements to 0, drops the packet, and sends ICMP Time Exceeded to the source.
hints: Router decrements TTL by 1; TTL hits 0 = drop + ICMP Time Exceeded

questions.question: Which IPv4 header field identifies the transport protocol carried in the payload?
questions.explanation: The Protocol field (8 bits) in the IPv4 header identifies the encapsulated protocol: 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP, 89=OSPF, 47=GRE.
hints: IPv4 Protocol field: 6=TCP, 17=UDP, 1=ICMP, 89=OSPF

questions.question: What is the maximum number of VLANs supported by 802.1Q?
questions.explanation: 802.1Q uses a 12-bit VLAN ID field. 2^12 = 4096 possible values. IDs 0 and 4095 are reserved. Usable VLANs: 1–4094.
hints: 12-bit VLAN ID = 4096 total; 2 reserved = 4094 usable

questions.question: Which subnetting approach allows different subnets to have different sizes based on requirements?
questions.explanation: VLSM allows using different prefix lengths for different subnets in the same address space. A /30 for point-to-point, /25 for a 100-host LAN, /24 for a 200-host LAN — all from the same parent block.
hints: VLSM = different masks for different subnets; efficient IP usage

questions.question: A host sends an ARP request. What destination MAC address is used in the Ethernet frame?
questions.explanation: ARP request is sent as a Layer 2 broadcast (FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF) so all devices on the local segment receive and process it. Only the target host with the matching IP sends an ARP reply (unicast).
hints: ARP request = L2 broadcast; ARP reply = L2 unicast

questions.question: Which protocol is used to automatically assign IPv6 addresses to interfaces on a router (without DHCPv6)?
options: SLAAC combined with NDP Router Advertisements
questions.explanation: IPv6 interfaces on routers also use SLAAC: they receive RA prefixes and form addresses using EUI-64 or random IIDs. Alternatively, static or DHCPv6 can be used.
hints: SLAAC works for router interfaces too; RA provides the prefix

questions.question: What is the correct CIDR notation for the subnet 172.16.32.0 with mask 255.255.224.0?
What is the purpose of the IPv6 Duplicate Address Detection (DAD) process?
options: Before using an address, the host sends a Neighbor Solicitation to verify no other device on the link is already using it
DAD assigns a new address if the desired one is taken
DAD is used only by DHCPv6 servers
DAD is the IPv6 equivalent of ARP gratuitous ARP reply
questions.explanation: DAD uses NDP NS: the host sends a Neighbor Solicitation for its own tentative address. If another device claims it (sends an NA), the address is marked as duplicate and not used.
hints: DAD = NS for own address; if nobody replies = safe to use

questions.question: What is 'proxy ARP' and when is it used?
options: A router responds to ARP requests on behalf of remote hosts, allowing hosts with no default gateway to reach remote networks
Proxy ARP is used to resolve IPv6 addresses
Proxy ARP encrypts ARP replies for security
Proxy ARP is used only in MPLS networks
questions.explanation: Proxy ARP: a router responds to an ARP request for a remote IP with its own MAC address. The host sends traffic to the router's MAC, which then routes it. Cisco enables proxy ARP by default on Ethernet interfaces.
hints: Proxy ARP = router answers ARP for remote IPs; enables hosts without gateway config

questions.question: What is the network address and broadcast address for host 192.168.150.200/26?
questions.explanation: /26 = block 64 in last octet. 200/64=3.125 → block starts at 192 (3×64). Network=.192, broadcast=.255.
hints: Block size for /26 = 64; 200/64=3.125 → block at 3×64=192

questions.question: What is the purpose of the TCP PSH (Push) flag?
options: Instructs the receiver to pass the data to the application immediately without buffering, rather than waiting to fill the buffer
Pushes data directly without a 3-way handshake
Acknowledges the previous segment while simultaneously pushing new data
Used only during TCP connection teardown
questions.explanation: PSH: sender tells the receiver to deliver buffered data to the application immediately, without waiting for the TCP buffer to fill. Used for interactive applications like SSH where immediate delivery matters.
hints: PSH = deliver data to application now; don't wait for buffer to fill

questions.question: What is a MAC address 'burned-in address' (BIA)?
options: The factory-programmed, globally unique 48-bit hardware address in the NIC's ROM
A MAC address configured by the OS software
A virtual MAC assigned to a VLAN interface
The default gateway's MAC address stored in ARP cache
questions.explanation: BIA: the permanent MAC address programmed by the manufacturer into the NIC's hardware. First 3 bytes = OUI (vendor), last 3 bytes = device-specific. Can be overridden in software (MAC spoofing).
hints: BIA = factory MAC in ROM; OUI = first 3 bytes (vendor)

questions.question: Which IP address is used as the source when a router sends an ICMP Time Exceeded message?
options: The IP address of the interface that received and discarded the packet (or the egress interface)
The original source IP of the dropped packet
questions.explanation: ICMP error messages are sent from the router's interface IP (typically the interface that received the packet). The router uses the interface address as the ICMP source.
hints: ICMP Time Exceeded source = router's interface IP (reveals the hop in traceroute)

questions.question: An Ethernet frame contains a destination MAC of 01:00:5e:xx:xx:xx. What type of frame is this?
options: IPv4 multicast frame — the MAC maps to an IPv4 multicast group address
A unicast frame to a specific server
questions.explanation: 01:00:5e:xx:xx:xx is the IPv4 multicast MAC range. IANA maps IPv4 multicast addresses to this range (low-order 23 bits of the group address). IPv6 multicast MACs use 33:33:xx:xx:xx:xx.
hints: 01:00:5e = IPv4 multicast MAC; 33:33 = IPv6 multicast MAC

questions.question: Which command on a Cisco router clears the ARP cache?
questions.explanation: `clear arp-cache` removes all dynamic ARP entries from the router's ARP table. Forces re-ARP on next transmission. Can resolve issues caused by stale ARP entries.
hints: clear arp-cache = flush all dynamic ARP entries

questions.question: What is a summary route and what benefit does it provide?
options: A single route representing multiple specific routes — reduces routing table size, speeds up lookups, and reduces routing protocol update bandwidth
A route with higher administrative distance
A backup route used only when primary routes fail
questions.explanation: Route summarization (aggregation) combines multiple specific prefixes into one advertisement. Fewer routing table entries = faster lookup, less memory, smaller routing updates, and faster convergence.
hints: Summary = one route for many; reduces table size and update overhead

questions.question: Which transport protocol does TFTP use, and why?
options: UDP — TFTP is designed for simple file transfer in controlled environments where overhead reduction is more important than reliability
ICMP — TFTP uses ICMP echo for file chunking
questions.explanation: TFTP (Trivial FTP) uses UDP port 69. It has its own simple block acknowledgment mechanism but uses UDP for minimal overhead. Used for IOS image transfer, network booting (PXE), and config downloads.
hints: TFTP=UDP 69; simple, low overhead; used for IOS uploads

questions.question: How is the first usable host address of a subnet calculated?
questions.explanation: First usable host = network address + 1 (the address with host portion = all zeros + 1). Last usable host = broadcast - 1. Broadcast = network address + (block size - 1).
hints: First host = network+1; last host = broadcast-1

questions.question: Which IPv4 addressing class uses the first bit as 1, second bit as 1, third bit as 1, and fourth bit as 1 (binary 1111)?
questions.explanation: IPv4 classes by leading bits: A=0, B=10, C=110, D=1110 (multicast 224-239), E=1111 (experimental 240-255). Class E is reserved for experimental use.
hints: Class D=1110 (multicast); Class E=1111 (experimental)

questions.question: What is the network address of 10.25.128.0/18?
hints: Block size for /18 = 64; 128 is 2×64 → network boundary

questions.question: How many host bits does a /29 mask leave for host addresses?
questions.explanation: /29 = 32-29=3 host bits. 2^3-2=6 usable hosts per subnet.
hints: Host bits = 32 minus prefix; /29 → 3 host bits

questions.question: Which IPv4 address is in the same /24 subnet as 192.168.5.1?
questions.explanation: 192.168.5.254 is in 192.168.5.0/24 (same third octet = 5). 192.168.5.0 is the network address, not a valid host.
hints: Same /24 = same first three octets

questions.question: Which transport layer feature prevents out-of-order TCP segments from being delivered to the application out-of-sequence?
questions.explanation: TCP sequence numbers allow the receiver to buffer out-of-order segments and deliver them to the application in the correct order once gaps are filled.
hints: TCP resequencing: buffer + sequence numbers → in-order delivery to app

questions.question: What is the binary value of the decimal number 172?
What is the last usable host address in the subnet 172.31.64.0/18?
hints: Broadcast = next block - 1 = 172.31.128.0 - 1 = 172.31.127.255; last host = broadcast-1

questions.question: A network diagram shows two routers connected via an Ethernet link. Which protocol would you use to detect the Layer 2 neighbor and its interface details without manual configuration?
questions.explanation: CDP (Cisco) or LLDP (IEEE 802.1AB) automatically discovers directly connected neighbors and exchanges device capability, interface, and IP information at Layer 2.
hints: CDP/LLDP = automatic neighbor discovery; no routing protocol needed

questions.question: What does the subnet mask 255.255.255.252 (/30) restrict a subnet to?
options: 2 usable host addresses (ideal for point-to-point links)
questions.explanation: /30 = 2 host bits. 2^2-2=2 usable. Perfect for point-to-point links between two routers.
hints: /30 = 2 usable hosts; standard for WAN point-to-point links

questions.question: Which subnets are created when 192.168.10.0/24 is subnetted into /26 subnets?
questions.explanation: /26 block size=64. From /24: .0, .64, .128, .192 — four subnets.
hints: Block size /26 = 64; four subnets starting at 0, 64, 128, 192

questions.question: Which Ethernet standard supports 10 Gbps over multimode fiber at distances up to 300 meters?
questions.explanation: 10GBASE-SR: 850nm laser, OM3/OM4 multimode fiber, up to 300m. LR=10km single-mode. ER=40km. T=copper Cat6A up to 100m.
hints: SR=short reach (multimode, 300m); LR=long reach (single-mode, 10km)

questions.question: What type of transmission sends data to ALL nodes in a network simultaneously?
questions.explanation: Broadcast: sent to all hosts in a domain (L2 broadcast to FF:FF:FF:FF:FF:FF or L3 to 255.255.255.255). Multicast: sent to a group. Anycast: to the nearest one of a group.
hints: Broadcast=all; Multicast=group; Unicast=one; Anycast=nearest of group

questions.question: What is the first valid host address in the subnet 10.1.0.0/16?
questions.explanation: 10.1.0.0 is the network address. First valid host = 10.1.0.1.
hints: First host = network + 1; 10.1.0.0 is network address

questions.question: What is the purpose of the IPv4 header checksum field?
options: Verifies the integrity of the IP header only (not the payload); recalculated at each hop as TTL changes
Verifies both header and payload integrity
Provides authentication of the packet source
Acts as a sequence number for fragmented packets
questions.explanation: IPv4 header checksum covers only the header fields. Since TTL changes at every router, the checksum must be recalculated at each hop. IPv6 removed the header checksum (upper layers handle integrity).
hints: IP checksum = header-only; recalculated each hop; IPv6 removed it

questions.question: Which TCP flag combination is used to reset an established connection immediately?
questions.explanation: TCP RST: abruptly terminates a connection. No data drain. Used for error conditions or closing connections to closed ports. FIN+ACK is used in the graceful four-way teardown.
hints: RST = abrupt reset; FIN = graceful close

questions.question: A host sends data at 1 Gbps. After passing through a router to a 100 Mbps WAN link, what happens to excess traffic?
options: The router's output queue buffers it; if the queue fills, packets are dropped (tail drop or RED)
The router automatically slows the source using TCP backpressure
The router fragments packets to reduce their size
Excess traffic is returned to the sender
questions.explanation: When traffic arrives faster than the outbound interface can transmit, the output queue buffers it. When the queue is full, new packets are dropped (tail drop). QoS policies manage which packets are dropped first.
hints: Queue full = packet drop (tail drop); QoS = smarter drop/scheduling

questions.question: What is the 6-byte Ethernet frame preamble and SFD used for?
options: Allow receiving NICs to synchronize their clock to the sender before the frame data begins; SFD marks the start of the actual frame
Store the source and destination MAC addresses
Provide error detection for the frame
questions.explanation: Preamble (7 bytes alternating 1/0): clock sync. SFD (Start Frame Delimiter, 1 byte 10101011): marks the end of preamble and start of actual frame. Not included in the 64-byte minimum frame size calculation.
hints: Preamble+SFD = 8 bytes for clock sync before frame data; not counted in frame size

questions.question: Which statement about IGMP is correct?
options: IGMP allows hosts to join IPv4 multicast groups; routers use IGMP to track which interfaces have group members
IGMP is used by routers to advertise multicast routes
IGMP is the IPv6 multicast management protocol
IGMP eliminates the need for PIM
questions.explanation: IGMP (Internet Group Management Protocol): hosts send IGMP Join to their local router to request multicast traffic. Routers track memberships per interface using IGMP, then use PIM to build multicast trees.
hints: IGMP = host ↔ local router; PIM = router ↔ router for multicast routing

questions.question: What information does a DNS PTR record contain?
options: Maps an IP address back to a hostname (reverse DNS lookup)
Maps a hostname to an IPv4 address
Identifies a mail server for the domain
Provides load balancing by mapping one name to multiple IPs
questions.explanation: PTR (Pointer) record: IP → hostname reverse lookup. Stored in the in-addr.arpa zone (IPv4) or ip6.arpa zone (IPv6). Used by spam filters, log readability, and security tools.
hints: PTR = reverse DNS; IP → hostname

questions.question: What does a 'half-open' TCP connection mean?
options: A connection where the SYN has been received and SYN-ACK sent but the final ACK has not arrived yet
A connection with only one-way data flow
A connection using only half the TCP window
questions.explanation: Half-open (half-established): the server has replied with SYN-ACK and is waiting for the client's ACK to complete the handshake. SYN flood attacks exploit this by creating thousands of half-open connections.
hints: Half-open = SYN received, SYN-ACK sent, waiting for ACK; exploited by SYN flood

questions.question: What is the number of IPv4 addresses available in a /20 subnet?
questions.explanation: /20 = 12 host bits. 2^12 = 4096 total addresses (4094 usable).
hints: Total addresses = 2^(32-prefix) = 2^12 = 4096

questions.question: Which field in the TCP header contains the number of the next byte the sender expects to receive?
questions.explanation: ACK number: the next byte number the receiver expects. If the receiver has received bytes 0-999, the ACK number is 1000. The sender knows bytes through 999 are confirmed.
hints: ACK number = next expected byte; confirms receipt of all bytes before it

questions.question: A router has two static routes to the same destination with different ADs. Which statement is true?
options: Only the lower-AD route is installed in the routing table; the higher-AD route is a floating static used as backup
Both routes are installed and load-balanced
AD doesn't affect static route selection
questions.explanation: When two routes point to the same destination, lower AD wins. The higher-AD route is not installed. If the lower-AD route disappears (interface down), the floating static takes over.
hints: Lower AD = active route; higher AD = floating backup (inactive while primary exists)

questions.question: Which protocol provides Layer 2 loop prevention in a network with redundant switch links?
questions.explanation: STP/RSTP places redundant Layer 2 ports in blocking state to prevent loops. Only one active path exists between any two points. LACP bundles links (no loop), HSRP is gateway redundancy.
hints: STP = L2 loop prevention; blocks redundant paths

questions.question: What is the IP address range for the TEST-NET documentation block commonly used in examples?
questions.explanation: 192.0.2.0/24 is TEST-NET-1 (RFC 5737), used in documentation and examples. Also 198.51.100.0/24 and 203.0.113.0/24. Not routable on the internet.
hints: 192.0.2.0/24 = TEST-NET-1; appears in exam questions and RFC examples

questions.question: Which IPv6 address type is used for one-to-nearest delivery (routing to the closest interface with that address)?
questions.explanation: IPv6 Anycast: an address assigned to multiple interfaces; routing sends packets to the topologically nearest one. Used for load balancing and redundancy (e.g., DNS root servers, CDNs). IPv6 has no broadcast.
hints: Anycast = one-to-nearest; IPv6 unicast/multicast/anycast; no broadcast in IPv6

questions.question: What decimal value does 11111111 (8 binary ones) represent?
questions.explanation: 11111111 = 128+64+32+16+8+4+2+1 = 255. This is the value for a fully-masked octet in a subnet mask.

questions.question: A /16 subnet is split into /20 subnets. How many /20 subnets are created?
questions.explanation: /20 borrows 4 bits from /16 (20-16=4). 2^4=16 subnets.
hints: Borrowed bits = 20-16=4; subnets = 2^4 = 16

questions.question: What does 'connectionless' mean in the context of UDP?
options: No session is established before data is sent; each datagram is independent with no prior handshake
UDP cannot connect to remote hosts at all
UDP uses unreliable hardware connections only
UDP transmits data in a continuous stream without packetization
questions.explanation: Connectionless: UDP sends datagrams without first establishing a session (no SYN/SYN-ACK/ACK). Each datagram is processed independently by the receiver. Faster but unreliable.
hints: Connectionless = no handshake; each packet independent; no guaranteed delivery

questions.question: Which field in the Ethernet frame is used for error detection?
questions.explanation: FCS (4 bytes, CRC-32): the sender computes a CRC over the frame; the receiver recomputes and compares. If mismatch, the frame is dropped. FCS detects bit errors in transmission.
hints: FCS = CRC-based error detection; corrupt frame = dropped

questions.question: How many bits does an IPv4 address contain, and how is it typically written?
options: 32 bits, written as four octets in dotted decimal (e.g., 192.168.1.1)
questions.explanation: IPv4: 32 bits total, divided into four 8-bit octets written in dotted decimal notation (0-255 per octet). IPv6 is 128 bits.
hints: IPv4=32 bits=4 octets dotted decimal; IPv6=128 bits=8 hex groups

questions.scenario_text: A network engineer receives a complaint that TCP connections from PC-A (192.168.1.10) to a web server (10.0.0.5) are failing intermittently. Using Wireshark, she captures traffic and observes TCP SYN packets leaving the NIC but no SYN-ACK returning.

sub_questions.question: At which OSI layer does a TCP SYN segment exist before Ethernet encapsulation adds the frame header?
sub_questions.explanation: TCP is a Layer 4 (Transport) protocol. The segment exists at Layer 4 before Layer 2 encapsulation wraps it in an Ethernet frame.

sub_questions.question: What is the PDU name for data at Layer 2 of the OSI model?
sub_questions.explanation: PDU names: L1=Bit, L2=Frame, L3=Packet, L4=Segment, L5-7=Data.

sub_questions.question: A router processes PDUs up to OSI Layer 3 only, stripping the Layer 2 header to make forwarding decisions.
sub_questions.explanation: Routers operate at L3. They strip the incoming L2 frame, read the L3 IP header to determine the next hop, then re-encapsulate in a new L2 frame for the outgoing interface.

questions.scenario_text: A company runs two critical applications: VoIP calls and large file transfers. The network team is designing QoS policies and needs to understand the transport protocols each application uses to make appropriate queuing decisions.

sub_questions.question: VoIP applications typically use which transport protocol, and for what reason?
options: TCP — guaranteed delivery ensures voice quality
UDP — low overhead is preferred; retransmission delays would worsen call quality
TCP — the three-way handshake synchronises call endpoints
UDP — it provides built-in flow control for voice streams
sub_questions.explanation: VoIP uses UDP. Voice is delay-sensitive; a retransmitted late packet would cause more disruption than a missing one. UDP's lower overhead and no retransmission mechanism suit real-time media.

sub_questions.question: Which TCP mechanism prevents a fast sender from overwhelming a slow receiver?
sub_questions.explanation: TCP flow control uses a receive window (advertised in every TCP header). The sender cannot send more unacknowledged data than the receiver's window allows.

sub_questions.question: UDP provides acknowledgement and retransmission of lost segments, just like TCP.
sub_questions.explanation: UDP is connectionless with no acknowledgement, retransmission, sequencing, or flow control. Applications that need reliability must implement it themselves above UDP.

questions.scenario_text: A technician is configuring a new subnet for 50 hosts in a branch office. She has been allocated the 192.168.10.0 address block and must choose the smallest subnet mask that accommodates all 50 hosts.

sub_questions.question: Which prefix length provides enough space for 50 hosts with the least address waste?
sub_questions.explanation: /26 gives 2^6 − 2 = 62 usable hosts, which covers 50. /27 only gives 30, which is insufficient. /25 works but wastes 76 addresses. /26 is the best fit.

sub_questions.question: For subnet 192.168.10.64/26, what is the broadcast address?
sub_questions.explanation: 192.168.10.64/26 spans .64–.127. The broadcast is the last address: 192.168.10.127.

sub_questions.question: The network address 192.168.10.64 can be assigned to a host interface on the /26 subnet.
sub_questions.explanation: 192.168.10.64 is the network (subnet) address for the /26 subnet. It cannot be assigned to a host. Usable host range is .65–.126.

questions.scenario_text: A Cisco Catalyst switch is operating normally. A new host connects to an access port and sends its first Ethernet frame destined for a server whose MAC address is not yet in the switch's MAC address table.

sub_questions.question: When the switch receives a frame with an unknown destination MAC address, what does it do?
options: Floods the frame out all ports except the ingress port
Sends an ICMP error back to the source
Forwards the frame only to the default gateway
sub_questions.explanation: When a switch has no MAC table entry for the destination, it floods the frame out every port in the same VLAN except the port it arrived on (unknown unicast flooding).

sub_questions.question: How does the switch learn the MAC address of the new host?
options: By examining the destination MAC of frames the host sends
By examining the source MAC of frames the host sends
By sending an ARP request to the host
By consulting a DHCP binding table
sub_questions.explanation: Switches learn MAC addresses by reading the source MAC field of incoming frames and associating it with the ingress port in the MAC address table.

sub_questions.question: What is the standard maximum payload (MTU) of an Ethernet II frame?
sub_questions.explanation: The Ethernet II MTU (maximum payload) is 1500 bytes. The maximum frame size including header and FCS is 1518 bytes (or 1522 with 802.1Q tag).

questions.scenario_text: A company is migrating to IPv6. The engineer assigns the address 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334/64 to a router interface and needs to communicate the address to the team in shortened form.

sub_questions.question: What is the fully compressed form of 2001:0db8:85a3:0000:0000:8a2e:0370:7334?
sub_questions.explanation: Two consecutive all-zero groups (0000:0000) are compressed to ::. Leading zeros are removed from each group. The :: can appear only once. Result: 2001:db8:85a3::8a2e:370:7334.

sub_questions.question: If no static address is configured, EUI-64 generates an interface identifier from which source?
sub_questions.explanation: EUI-64 splits the 48-bit MAC into two 24-bit halves, inserts FF:FE in the middle, and flips the universal/local bit to create a 64-bit interface ID.

sub_questions.question: An IPv6 link-local address begins with FE80::/10 and is automatically generated on every IPv6-enabled interface.
sub_questions.explanation: All IPv6 interfaces automatically generate a link-local address in the FE80::/10 range. Link-local addresses are used for on-link communication, neighbor discovery, and routing protocol packets.

questions.question: What is the CIA Triad in information security?
questions.explanation: CIA Triad: Confidentiality (only authorized access), Integrity (data not altered in transit), Availability (systems accessible when needed). The foundation of information security policy.

questions.question: Which type of firewall operates at Layer 7 and can inspect HTTP content, block specific URLs, and detect application signatures?
questions.explanation: NGFW (Next-Gen Firewalls) like Cisco Firepower operate at Layer 7 with deep packet inspection (DPI), application identification, URL filtering, and IPS capabilities — far beyond stateful packet inspection.

questions.question: At which OSI layer does a router primarily make forwarding decisions?
questions.explanation: Routers operate at Layer 3 (Network) and forward packets based on destination IP addresses in their routing table.
distractor_notes: Layer 1 devices (hubs, repeaters) just regenerate signals — they have no concept of addressing.
Switches operate at Layer 2 using MAC addresses; they switch frames within a broadcast domain, not between networks.
Layer 4 (TCP/UDP) handles end-to-end reliability and port numbers — routers don't inspect TCP headers to make forwarding decisions.

questions.question: Which statement best explains why TCP is considered reliable while UDP is not?
options: TCP uses a checksum; UDP does not
TCP uses acknowledgements and retransmission; UDP does not
TCP is encrypted; UDP is sent in plaintext
TCP segments are smaller than UDP datagrams
questions.explanation: TCP reliability comes from its acknowledgement (ACK) mechanism and retransmission of lost segments. UDP has no acknowledgement or retransmission.
distractor_notes: Both TCP and UDP include a checksum for error detection — but detecting an error is not the same as recovering from it via retransmission.
Encryption is handled by TLS, which works over both TCP and UDP — neither protocol is inherently encrypted.
Segment size is unrelated to reliability — large TCP segments are still reliably delivered via acknowledgements.

questions.question: Which range defines the Class B private IP address space (RFC 1918)?
questions.explanation: The Class B private range is 172.16.0.0/12, covering 172.16.0.0–172.31.255.255 — 16 consecutive /16 networks.
distractor_notes: 10.0.0.0/8 is the Class A private range — a single /8 with over 16 million addresses.
A common mistake — the private range starts at 172.16, not 172.0. Addresses 172.0–172.15 are public.
192.168.0.0/16 is the Class C private range — the smallest of the three RFC 1918 blocks.

questions.question: A switch port in full-duplex mode can send and receive simultaneously. Which issue does this eliminate?
questions.explanation: Full-duplex uses separate transmit and receive paths, eliminating collisions. CSMA/CD is disabled in full-duplex because simultaneous send/receive cannot produce collisions.
distractor_notes: Broadcast storms are caused by Layer 2 loops — they are prevented by STP, not by changing the duplex mode.
MAC table overflow is a CAM overflow attack — duplex mode has no effect on the MAC address table.
Spanning tree loops are a topology issue — STP prevents them regardless of whether ports run half- or full-duplex.

questions.question: In subnetting, what is the correct formula for the number of usable host addresses in a /<n> subnet?
questions.explanation: The host portion has (32-n) bits, giving 2^(32-n) total addresses. Subtract 2 for network and broadcast to get usable hosts.

questions.question: Which OSI layer is responsible for logical addressing and path determination?
questions.explanation: Layer 3 (Network) handles IP addressing and routing — choosing the best path across networks.

source_ref.text: Jeremy's IT Lab — OSI Model & TCP/IP Suite
